proposals.title: Factual Self-Correction via Preference Optimization: Reducing Annotation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP
proposals.motivation: The assumption that high-performance NLP requires vast, manually annotated corpora is a major bottleneck, especially for tasks requiring factual accuracy and knowledge grounding. While large language models (LLMs) encode substantial knowledge, their outputs can be inconsistent or factually flawed, and correcting these flaws typically demands expensive human feedback or curated datasets. This work challenges that paradigm by exploring how models can self-improve factual consistency with minimal direct annotation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a language model can be guided to produce more factually consistent multi-hop responses through an iterative, self-supervised process that uses its own internal representations as a source of preference signals, dramatically reducing the need for external human feedback.
proposals.method: We propose a meta-learning framework where a base LLM is adapted online using a novel combination of methods. First, we employ a MQuAKE-style probe to identify factual inconsistencies in the model's own generations on multi-hop questions. These inconsistent generations, paired with corrected versions derived from the model's own parametric knowledge (retrieved via constrained decoding), create synthetic preference pairs. We then apply Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) on these self-generated pairs, iteratively refining the model. The process focuses on editing and reinforcing specific factual pathways without full retraining.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new methodology for sample-efficient alignment of LLMs towards factual accuracy, reducing reliance on large-scale human preference data. It will provide empirical evidence on the limits and potential of self-correction for knowledge editing and consistency. The resulting framework could lower the barrier to deploying reliable, knowledge-grounded models in specialized domains.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating knowledge editing assessment (MQuAKE) with offline preference optimization (DPO) in a closed-loop, self-supervised meta-learning scheme. This moves beyond using RLHF with human labels or synthetic data from other models, instead leveraging the model's own knowledge as its training signal.

proposals.title: Speech-First Language Models: Foundational Pre-training and Adaptation from Raw Audio
proposals.motivation: Modern large language models are built on a text-centric paradigm, inheriting its abstractions and limitations. This ignores the primacy of speech in human language acquisition and communication, which is grounded in a rich, continuous signal containing prosody, emotion, and disfluencies. Developing foundational models directly from speech could lead to more natural, efficient, and human-aligned AI systems, particularly for low-resource languages and applications like interactive assistants.
proposals.hypothesis: A transformer-based model, pre-trained directly on raw speech waveforms or learned audio tokens without textual supervision, can learn a latent linguistic structure sufficient for downstream language understanding and generation tasks when fine-tuned with minimal text.
proposals.method: We will adapt architectures like wav2vec 2.0 and HuBERT for causal, generative modeling, pre-training on massive, multilingual speech corpora (e.g., LibriLight, Common Voice) using masked prediction and contrastive losses. The core innovation is a two-stage adaptation: first, using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) with human auditory feedback on generated speech to shape prosody and coherence; second, employing meta-learning techniques to rapidly adapt the speech-based latent representations to specific text-based tasks, effectively 'grounding' text in the primary speech model.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first large-scale, generative 'speech-foundation' model and a framework for adapting it. It will provide empirical evidence on the linguistic sufficiency of speech signals and introduce new evaluation benchmarks for speech-centric knowledge and reasoning, moving beyond transcribed text.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that converts speech to text as a first step, this proposal inverts the pipeline, treating text as a derived, specialized modality. It uniquely combines speech-only pre-training with RLHF/DPO for speech generation and meta-learning for efficient cross-modal adaptation, a synthesis not yet explored.

proposals.title: Integrated Language Processing: A Joint Architecture for Continuous Parsing, Disambiguation, and Factual Retrieval
proposals.motivation: Traditional NLP pipelines treat parsing, semantic disambiguation, and knowledge retrieval as sequential, independent modules, which can lead to error propagation and an inability to revise early decisions based on later context. This is particularly problematic for tasks requiring deep language understanding and factual grounding, as seen in challenges like multi-hop QA and knowledge editing. A more integrated, continuous interaction between these levels is needed to build more robust and context-aware language models.
proposals.hypothesis: A neural architecture that jointly and continuously performs syntactic parsing, semantic disambiguation, and external knowledge retrieval within a single forward pass will outperform cascaded, sequential systems on tasks requiring deep language understanding and factual consistency.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel Transformer-based architecture with specialized, interleaved attention heads and a dynamic routing mechanism. A structured-prediction attention head will produce incremental syntactic parses, while a separate 'retrieval head' will use these parses and contextual embeddings to query a dense vector index of a knowledge base (e.g., Wikipedia) in real-time. The retrieved facts will be fused back into the model's hidden states via a gating mechanism, influencing subsequent disambiguation and parsing decisions, creating a closed-loop, continuous interaction. We will train this model end-to-end using a combination of supervised parsing data and Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) on downstream QA and fact-updating tasks (inspired by MQuAKE).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new architectural paradigm for language models that tightly integrates formal linguistic structure with neural representation and factual knowledge. It will yield a model capable of more transparent and reliable reasoning, directly advancing research in interpretability, knowledge editing, and robust QA. The proposed system will serve as a testbed for studying the interaction between different levels of language processing.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has integrated retrieval into LMs (e.g., RAG) or used syntactic biases, no model before 2026 performs continuous, joint inference where parsing decisions directly trigger and are revised by real-time factual retrieval within a single cohesive architecture, moving beyond the clean separation of levels.

proposals.title: Online Adaptation for Non-Stationary Language: A Meta-Learning and DPO Framework for Temporal Robustness
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) are trained on static snapshots of data, leading to factual obsolescence and performance decay as language, knowledge, and user preferences evolve. This non-stationarity is a fundamental challenge for real-world deployment, where models must remain accurate and aligned over time. Existing methods like knowledge editing (e.g., MQuAKE) address isolated facts but lack a principled framework for continuous adaptation to shifting distributions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a language model equipped with a meta-learned adaptation mechanism, fine-tuned via Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) on temporally-stratified preference data, can efficiently and robustly track language evolution, outperforming static models and conventional fine-tuning on temporal generalization tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage framework. First, we will meta-train a base model on a curriculum of temporally-ordered tasks to acquire parameters that facilitate fast online adaptation. Second, we will apply Direct Preference Optimization using pairs of model responses to queries from different time periods, where preferences are defined by correctness relative to the temporal context (e.g., a 2024 answer is preferred over a 2020 answer for a 2024 fact). This combines gradient-based meta-learning for rapid parameter adjustment and DPO for stable alignment to evolving human judgments, evaluated on dynamic benchmarks like updated MQuAKE datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified method for continuous LLM adaptation, mitigating temporal degradation. It will contribute a new benchmark for evaluating temporal robustness and demonstrate how alignment techniques like DPO can be repurposed for temporal preference learning. The resulting models will be more sustainable and useful in dynamic information environments.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning and DPO exist separately, their integration for explicit temporal adaptation is novel. The approach moves beyond editing discrete facts to modeling the continuous drift of language and preference distributions, a core challenge not yet addressed by current adaptation or alignment techniques.

proposals.title: Meta-Learning Factual Adaptation for Low-Resource Language Models via DPO and Knowledge Editing
proposals.motivation: Statistical NLP for low-resource languages is hindered by limited data for both model training and subsequent knowledge updates. Current large language models (LLMs) often perform poorly on such languages, and fine-tuning them risks catastrophic forgetting of general capabilities while providing insufficient data for reliable factual grounding. This work addresses the dual challenge of adapting models to low-resource linguistic contexts while ensuring they can be precisely updated with factual knowledge, a critical need for equitable AI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-learning framework, which conditions a model's adaptation mechanism on language identity and sparse factual signals, can enable more parameter-efficient and factually stable fine-tuning for low-resource languages compared to standard fine-tuning or prompt-based methods.
proposals.method: We will extend meta-learning algorithms for online adaptation (inspired by 'Meta-Learning Online Adaptation of Language Models') to learn language-specific initialization vectors or soft prompts. The adaptation will be guided by Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), using synthetically generated preference pairs that reward factual correctness (assessed against a small curated seed set) and grammaticality in the target language. To manage factual updates without full retraining, we will integrate and evaluate knowledge editing techniques, like those analyzed in MQuAKE, to modify the model's internal representations for specific facts post-adaptation, measuring success via multi-hop question answering in the low-resource language.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, unified framework for parameter-efficient low-resource language adaptation that explicitly optimizes for factual reliability, 2) an empirical analysis of how knowledge editing techniques perform in low-data, cross-lingual settings, and 3) new benchmarks for evaluating factual consistency in adapted low-resource language models.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in combining meta-learning for language adaptation with DPO-driven factual alignment and rigorously testing post-adaptation knowledge editing, a synthesis not explored in existing low-resource NLP or model editing literature pre-2026.

proposals.title: Inverse Factual Control: Editing Language Models for Faithful, Multi-Hop Text Generation
proposals.motivation: While large language models (LLMs) are powerful generators, they often produce text containing factual errors or hallucinations. Current knowledge editing methods, like those assessed in MQuAKE, focus on updating a model's internal knowledge for QA tasks. The inverse problem—using an edited model to generate fluent, multi-sentence narratives that consistently reflect a specific set of updated facts—remains a significant, underexplored challenge for controllable text generation.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a meta-learned adaptation mechanism with a Direct Preference Optimization (DPO)-based objective for factual consistency, a language model can be steered to generate coherent, multi-hop text that is both stylistically natural and faithful to a dynamically edited set of factual constraints.
proposals.method: We will build on the MQuAKE framework for multi-hop knowledge editing and the DPO algorithm for aligning model outputs. The core method involves: 1) Using a meta-learning setup (inspired by 'Meta-Learning Online Adaptation') to enable rapid adaptation of a base LM to a set of edited facts. 2) Applying DPO, where the reward model is defined by faithfulness to these edited facts (evaluated via an NLI model or retrieval), to fine-tune the adapted model for generation. 3) Employing constrained decoding or gradient-based guidance during generation to ensure the output text remains on the factual 'manifold' defined by the edits.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel pipeline for factually constrained text generation, moving beyond knowledge editing for QA to controlled narrative construction. It will provide new benchmarks for evaluating factual consistency in generated text and demonstrate how DPO can be repurposed for explicit factual control, not just stylistic preference.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in framing knowledge editing as an inverse problem for controlled generation and in the specific integration of meta-learning for rapid factual adaptation with DPO for optimizing factual—rather than stylistic—alignment during text generation.

proposals.title: Ensemble Alignment: Training Architecturally Diverse Models for Complementary Preferences via DPO
proposals.motivation: Current alignment research, dominated by transformer-based LLMs, risks architectural monoculture and homogenized model behavior. This limits robustness and fails to explore how different neural architectures might inherently capture distinct facets of human preference, such as factual grounding (RNNs/SSMs) versus creative coherence (transformers). A systematic investigation into aligning diverse architectures is crucial for building more efficient and resilient AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to an ensemble of architecturally diverse, smaller models (e.g., Mamba SSMs, modern RNNs, and compact transformers) will encourage them to specialize in different dimensions of preference, and a learned router can dynamically combine these specialized outputs to outperform a single, homogenously aligned model of comparable total size.
proposals.method: We will train a fixed ensemble of 3-4 small, diverse models (e.g., a Mamba SSM, a Linear Recurrent Unit model, a hybrid model, and a small transformer) on the same preference dataset using a shared DPO loss, but with a regularization term penalizing output similarity between ensemble members. A lightweight, per-token routing network will be trained concurrently via reinforcement learning, using a reward signal based on final output quality, to learn to weight the contributions of each specialized model dynamically.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, efficient ensemble method for aligned language generation and provide the first systematic analysis of how DPO and human preferences generalize across non-transformer architectures. It will advance the understanding of alignment as a property of diverse model ecosystems, not just individual models.
proposals.novelty: While DPO and ensembles are known, using DPO not to unify but to *diversify* the behavior of architecturally heterogeneous models is a novel direction. The integration of a learned router trained alongside the DPO process to exploit this induced specialization is also new.

proposals.title: Direct Preference Optimization for Non-Transformer Language Models: A Study on State Space Models and RWKV
proposals.motivation: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) has emerged as a powerful, stable method for aligning transformer-based LLMs with human preferences. However, its efficacy and behavior have not been systematically studied on the emerging class of efficient non-transformer architectures, such as State Space Models (e.g., Mamba) and linear attention variants (e.g., RWKV). Understanding if DPO's benefits generalize is crucial for the broader field of efficient, aligned language model development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the DPO loss function and training procedure can be successfully applied to non-transformer architectures, effectively aligning them with human preferences, but that the optimization dynamics and final model characteristics will differ meaningfully from transformer-based counterparts.
proposals.method: We will implement DPO training on pre-trained base models of two architectures: the Mamba state space model and the RWKV model. Using a fixed preference dataset (e.g., Anthropic HH), we will train aligned versions of each. We will rigorously compare the training stability, sample efficiency, and final performance of these models against a transformer baseline (e.g., Pythia) using automated metrics (e.g., GPT-4 preference scoring) and benchmarks like HellaSwag and TruthfulQA, while also analyzing the learned reward landscapes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical evidence for the applicability of DPO to leading non-transformer architectures, expanding the toolkit for their alignment. It will yield practical guidelines for tuning hyperparameters and diagnosing failures specific to these models, and may reveal architectural biases in how preferences are internalized.
proposals.novelty: While DPO is established for transformers, its application to structurally different, recurrent-like architectures such as Mamba and RWKV is unexplored. This tests the architectural agnosticism of a core alignment algorithm and intersects with the growing interest in sub-quadratic sequence models.

proposals.title: Factually Grounded Multi-Hop Reasoning via Cross-Modal Preference Optimization
proposals.motivation: Current large language models (LLMs) struggle with factual grounding and multi-step reasoning when questions require synthesizing information from diverse, non-textual sources like images, tables, and text. This limits their reliability in real-world applications such as scientific analysis or business intelligence, where answers are derived from heterogeneous data. Existing multi-modal models often treat modalities as supplementary rather than as co-equal, interdependent sources for complex inference.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model trained with a novel, fact-aware Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) objective over chains of cross-modal reasoning will significantly improve the factual accuracy and robustness of multi-hop answers compared to standard fine-tuning or naive RLHF.
proposals.method: We will extend the MQuAKE framework to create a benchmark for multi-hop, multi-modal factual reasoning. Building on a pre-trained vision-language model (e.g., Flamingo or BLIP-2), we will generate reasoning chains that explicitly cite evidence from each modality. The core innovation is a two-stage training process: first, supervised fine-tuning on chain-of-thought data, followed by Direct Preference Optimization where preferences are based on the factual consistency of the final answer with the cited evidence across all modalities, not just surface fluency.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a new benchmark and evaluation protocol for factually grounded multi-modal reasoning, 2) a demonstrated method (Cross-Modal DPO) that improves factual fidelity in complex QA, and 3) insights into how preference-based learning can be tailored for evidence-based reasoning over heterogeneous data.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating the knowledge-editing evaluation rigor of MQuAKE with the efficient alignment technique of DPO, specifically adapted to optimize for cross-modal factual consistency rather than general human preference, which is a non-obvious fusion of the researcher's recent themes.

proposals.title: Generating Coherent Multi-Hop Explanations via Fact-Guided Direct Preference Optimization
proposals.motivation: Current models for multi-hop reasoning, such as those evaluated by MQuAKE, primarily retrieve or extract discrete supporting facts. This limits their ability to produce fluent, free-form explanations that are crucial for user trust and educational applications. A method for generating open-ended, multi-step reasoning chains would bridge the gap between factual recall and coherent explanatory communication.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a language model fine-tuned with a hybrid objective—combining factual grounding on structured knowledge with Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) on explanation quality—can generate more factually faithful and coherent multi-hop explanations than models trained solely on extraction or standard RLHF.
proposals.method: We will build on a meta-learned base model (inspired by 'Meta-Learning Online Adaptation') to quickly adapt to new explanation tasks. The core method involves a two-stage fine-tuning process: first, supervised fine-tuning on datasets of multi-hop questions paired with gold explanation chains. Second, we will apply DPO, using a reward model trained to jointly score factual consistency (verified against a knowledge source) and explanatory coherence (e.g., logical flow, clarity), moving beyond simple sentence retrieval.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for open-ended explanation generation that prioritizes both factual accuracy and narrative coherence. It will also provide a new benchmark and evaluation suite for multi-hop explanation generation, extending beyond the editing-focused assessments of MQuAKE to measure the quality of generated reasoning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating DPO—typically used for aligning model outputs with human preferences on style or safety—with explicit factual grounding objectives for multi-step explanatory reasoning, a combination not explored in prior knowledge editing or preference optimization work.

proposals.title: Fact-Supervised Preference Optimization: Training Language Models for Verifiable and Explainable Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Current large language models (LLMs) often generate plausible but unverifiable or factually inconsistent reasoning chains, limiting their trustworthiness in knowledge-intensive tasks. While methods like RLHF and DPO align models with human preferences, they lack explicit grounding in verifiable supporting facts, which is crucial for multi-hop reasoning and factual accuracy. This project addresses the core challenge of making model reasoning transparent and factually grounded, a key step towards reliable AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly supervising a language model's internal reasoning process with sentence-level supporting facts, integrated into a direct preference optimization framework, will produce models that generate more accurate, verifiable, and explainable predictions for complex, multi-hop question answering.
proposals.method: We will develop Fact-Supervised DPO (FS-DPO), extending Direct Preference Optimization to incorporate supporting fact supervision. For a given question, the model will generate a reasoning chain alongside citations to provided factual sentences. The reward model will be a hybrid function combining (1) the standard preference score on the final answer and (2) a factual grounding score based on the precision and recall of cited sentences against gold supporting facts. We will train and evaluate on datasets like HotpotQA, using MQuAKE's methodology for assessing multi-hop knowledge editing as a key evaluation for robustness and factual stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel training paradigm that tightly couples reasoning alignment with factual verifiability. It will produce models whose predictions are more interpretable and factually consistent, advancing the field of explainable AI. The resulting techniques and analysis will provide new insights into how explicit fact supervision alters model internal representations and reasoning dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses supporting facts for post-hoc explanation or evaluation, we integrate them directly as a supervisory signal within a state-of-the-art alignment algorithm (DPO). This moves beyond optimizing for final answer preference alone to jointly optimize for the factual correctness of the entire reasoning pathway.

proposals.title: Fact-Consistent Explanation Generation via Meta-Learned Preference Optimization
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) often generate plausible but unfaithful explanations, hallucinating supporting facts. This undermines trust and limits their use in high-stakes domains like medicine or science. Existing methods, such as retrieval-augmented generation, improve factual grounding but do not explicitly optimize for the quality and consistency of the generated natural language justification itself.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model can be meta-trained to adaptively retrieve and integrate supporting facts, then optimized with a fact-aware Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) loss to produce explanations that are both human-readable and strictly faithful to the provided evidence.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage system. First, a meta-learning component, inspired by 'Meta-Learning Online Adaptation of Language Models', will enable the model to quickly adapt its retrieval and integration strategy for a given query-evidence set. Second, we will apply a novel DPO variant, where the reward model is conditioned not just on answer preference but on multi-hop factual consistency, using datasets like MQuAKE for evaluation. The training will fine-tune a base LM to maximize the likelihood of explanations preferred by this fact-conditioned reward model.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a new benchmark for evaluating the factual consistency of generated explanations in multi-hop reasoning settings, and 2) a demonstrated method that combines meta-learning for evidence use with DPO for explanation quality, advancing towards more trustworthy and transparent LLMs.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the integration of meta-learning for dynamic evidence retrieval with a fact-conditional DPO objective, moving beyond standard fine-tuning or RAG to directly optimize for the joint criteria of fluency and evidential faithfulness in explanation generation.

proposals.title: Joint Training for Answer and Support: A Multi-Task Framework for Verifiable and Interpretable Question Answering
proposals.motivation: Current large language models (LLMs) often generate answers without explicit grounding in source information, leading to issues with factual hallucination and a lack of interpretability. This is a critical barrier to deploying LLMs in high-stakes domains like medicine, law, and science, where verifying the provenance of an answer is as important as the answer itself. Existing retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and knowledge editing methods like MQuAKE address parts of this problem but treat answer generation and fact attribution as largely separate processes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model jointly trained to predict an answer and identify its supporting facts will produce more accurate, consistent, and verifiable outputs than models trained for either task in isolation, as the joint objective enforces internal alignment between the generated text and its evidential basis.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-task transformer architecture with two distinct output heads: one for answer generation (a decoder) and one for support identification (a span selector or token classifier over the provided context). The model will be trained on datasets like HotpotQA and FEVER with a combined loss: a standard cross-entropy loss for answer generation and a supervised contrastive or binary cross-entropy loss for support fact classification. We will explore fine-tuning a pre-trained model (e.g., T5 or LLaMA) and applying Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) on a reward function that jointly scores answer correctness and support precision, creating a unified RLHF loop for verifiability.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel training framework that intrinsically links answer generation to fact attribution, advancing the field of interpretable and trustworthy NLP. It will provide new benchmarks and metrics for evaluating the factual consistency of model outputs, directly addressing concerns raised by knowledge editing research. The resulting models will serve as a stronger foundation for applications requiring audit trails and reliable information retrieval.
proposals.novelty: While multi-task learning is well-established, its application to tightly couple final-answer generation and explicit support identification within a single, end-to-end fine-tuning and RLHF process is novel. This moves beyond pipeline or post-hoc attribution methods, aiming to bake verifiability directly into the model's reasoning process.

proposals.title: Graph Reasoning Networks: Enhancing Factual Consistency and Multi-Hop Reasoning in Language Models via Structured Knowledge Propagation
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) often struggle with factual consistency and multi-hop reasoning, as evidenced by challenges in knowledge editing benchmarks like MQuAKE. While fine-tuning and RLHF (e.g., DPO) improve general alignment, they do not explicitly model the relational structure between facts, leading to incoherent updates and reasoning failures. A method that explicitly represents and reasons over structured knowledge is needed to improve robustness and traceability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling documents and entities as a graph and using Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to propagate and refine information along reasoning paths will significantly improve the factual consistency and multi-hop reasoning capabilities of language models, outperforming purely parametric updates.
proposals.method: We will construct a heterogeneous graph from a corpus, with nodes for documents and entities (linked via mentions) and edges for semantic and relational connections. A GNN encoder (e.g., Graph Attention Network) will process this graph to generate updated node representations. For a given query, we will extract a relevant subgraph, use the GNN to perform multi-hop reasoning, and fuse the graph-derived representations with a frozen LLM's internal states via cross-attention. The system will be trained end-to-end using a combination of a multi-hop QA objective (on datasets like HotpotQA) and a contrastive loss for factual consistency, inspired by meta-learning for online adaptation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel neuro-symbolic architecture that integrates structured graph reasoning with LLMs, providing a more robust mechanism for knowledge-intensive tasks. It will offer a new benchmark for evaluating factual reasoning and a potential pathway for more reliable and interpretable knowledge updates in models, moving beyond purely parametric editing.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct integration of a trainable GNN-based reasoning module into the LLM inference loop for dynamic knowledge retrieval and fusion, contrasting with prior work that uses graphs only for retrieval or post-hoc verification. This approach explicitly trains the model to reason over structured paths, addressing a core limitation identified in knowledge editing research.

proposals.title: Multi-Task Adaptation for Robust Multi-Hop Reasoning: Joint Training on QA, Entailment, and Semantic Parsing
proposals.motivation: Current large language models (LLMs) still struggle with robust, compositional reasoning, as evidenced by challenges in knowledge editing benchmarks like MQuAKE. Multi-hop question answering (QA) tasks like HotpotQA require integrating and reasoning over multiple facts, a capability that can be brittle when models are fine-tuned narrowly. Pre-2026 work suggests that related tasks like textual entailment (NLI) and semantic parsing (e.g., QA-SRL, AMR) provide complementary supervision for structure and logic, which could improve generalization and factual consistency in QA.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-task learning (MTL) framework, jointly training a single model on HotpotQA, natural language inference, and semantic parsing tasks, will induce more robust and generalizable representations for multi-hop reasoning than single-task fine-tuning or sequential adaptation, leading to improved performance on out-of-distribution and fact-editing probes.
proposals.method: We will employ a unified encoder-decoder Transformer (e.g., T5 or FLAN-T5) as the base architecture. The model will be trained with a gradient-based MTL objective, combining losses from HotpotQA (answer generation), datasets like SNLI/MNLI (entailment classification), and semantic parsing tasks (sequence-to-structure generation). We will investigate hard parameter sharing with task-specific adapters (e.g., LoRA) and soft sharing via meta-learning techniques for online adaptation, inspired by prior work. The training protocol will carefully balance tasks using techniques like uncertainty weighting to mitigate negative interference.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a systematic analysis of which auxiliary tasks most effectively improve multi-hop reasoning robustness, 2) a new state-of-the-art model for HotpotQA with enhanced out-of-distribution generalization, and 3) a deeper understanding of how structured task supervision combats hallucination and improves factual precision, directly relevant to challenges in knowledge editing and RLHF.
proposals.novelty: While MTL is well-studied, its targeted application to *fortify* multi-hop reasoning against compositional errors and factual drift—explicitly connecting QA with structured semantic tasks—is novel. This approach moves beyond using NLI merely as a pretraining signal, instead treating it as a core, concurrent training objective for improving reasoning fidelity in the era of instruction-tuned LLMs.

proposals.title: Inverse MQuAKE: Synthesizing Multi-Hop Questions from Evidence Sets for Controlled Evaluation of Knowledge Integration
proposals.motivation: Current benchmarks for assessing knowledge editing and factual consistency in language models, like MQuAKE, rely on manually curated multi-hop questions. This process is costly, difficult to scale, and may not systematically cover the complex compositional reasoning spaces we need to evaluate. Automatically generating high-quality, multi-hop questions from evidence sets would enable the creation of large-scale, diverse, and targeted evaluation suites, accelerating research into model reliability and knowledge grounding.
proposals.hypothesis: A language model, fine-tuned via reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) or Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) on a seed corpus of (evidence, question) pairs, can learn to generate valid, non-trivial, and answerable multi-hop questions that require compositional reasoning across the provided documents.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage pipeline. First, we will create a training dataset by repurposing existing multi-hop QA datasets (e.g., HotpotQA) to form (evidence set, question) pairs and augmenting them with synthetic examples via prompting large LMs. Second, we will fine-tune a T5 or Llama-family model on this data, treating question generation as a conditional text generation task. The core innovation is a subsequent DPO fine-tuning stage, where a reward model—trained to score questions on criteria like answerability, multi-hop necessity, and fluency—will guide the model to produce higher-quality outputs, moving beyond simple supervised fine-tuning.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable, automated method for creating diagnostic benchmarks for knowledge-intensive NLP tasks. It will contribute a new model capability—controlled question generation from evidence—and release a generated dataset for evaluating knowledge integration and editing in LMs, directly supporting research like that in MQuAKE and meta-learning for adaptation.
proposals.novelty: While question generation is a known task, applying it specifically to create multi-hop questions from *sets* of evidence documents for the purpose of systematic model evaluation is novel. The integration of DPO for optimizing the nuanced, multi-faceted quality of generated questions, rather than just likelihood, represents a significant methodological advance over standard sequence-to-sequence approaches.

proposals.title: Adversarial Multi-Hop Question Generation via Counterfactual Reasoning for Robust Model Evaluation
proposals.motivation: Current benchmarks for evaluating knowledge editing and factual consistency in language models, like MQuAKE, rely on existing multi-hop questions that models may solve via spurious correlations or reasoning shortcuts. This leads to an overestimation of true reasoning and knowledge integration capabilities. A method to systematically generate adversarial questions that target these shortcuts is needed to create more rigorous evaluations and training objectives.
proposals.hypothesis: By using a counterfactual reasoning framework to generate multi-hop questions that deliberately violate common inference patterns, we can expose and quantify reasoning shortcuts in models fine-tuned with RLHF/DPO, leading to more robust evaluations and improved model training.
proposals.method: We will develop a generator model trained via meta-learning to produce adversarial questions. The method involves: 1) Using a base LM to generate candidate multi-hop questions from a knowledge graph, 2) Employing a 'shortcut probe' (a smaller model known to exploit surface patterns) to identify questions answerable via shortcuts, and 3) Applying a counterfactual editing step to these questions, altering surface forms or intermediate facts to create adversarial variants that preserve the final answer but break the shortcut. The generator's objective will be optimized using a reward based on the performance gap between a robust model and the shortcut probe.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel dataset of adversarial multi-hop questions for stress-testing model reasoning. It will provide a new diagnostic tool to evaluate the robustness of knowledge editing techniques and preference-tuning methods like DPO. The findings will inform the development of next-generation fine-tuning algorithms that target reasoning failures, not just factual errors.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., MQuAKE) assesses knowledge editing, it does not *generate* questions designed to exploit model-specific weaknesses. Our approach uniquely integrates counterfactual reasoning and meta-learning to create targeted adversarial examples, moving beyond static benchmarks to adaptive evaluation.

proposals.title: PolyGlot-Core: A Multilingual-First Foundation Model via Compositional, Typologically-Aware Pre-training and Fact-Aware RLHF
proposals.motivation: Current multilingual NLP systems are predominantly English-centric, built by extending English models with non-English data. This leads to suboptimal performance for many languages, especially low-resource ones, due to structural biases and inefficient parameter allocation. A toolkit designed from the ground up for linguistic diversity is crucial for equitable and robust global NLP applications.
proposals.hypothesis: A multilingual model architected from first principles with a compositionally modular design and typology-guided training will achieve superior cross-lingual generalization and factual consistency compared to English-extended models of similar scale.
proposals.method: We will design a transformer-based model with language-specific, sparsely activated modular components (e.g., mixture-of-experts) initialized and routed based on linguistic typological features (syntax, morphology). Pre-training will use a curated, balanced corpus across 50+ languages, employing a novel masked language modeling objective that encourages cross-module composition. Post-training will apply Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) with multilingual preference data, explicitly augmented for factual accuracy across languages, building on insights from MQuAKE to mitigate cross-lingual factual drift.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a state-of-the-art, publicly available multilingual foundation model and a reproducible framework for multilingual-first design. It will advance understanding of how to encode diverse linguistic structures efficiently in a single model and provide new benchmarks for cross-lingual factual consistency.
proposals.novelty: The approach moves beyond simple data mixing by hard-coding linguistic typology into the model's architecture and training dynamics from the start, and integrates fact-aware RLHF specifically for a multilingual context, a combination not explored in prior work.

proposals.title: Semantic Dependency Trees: Learning Task-Driven Hierarchies Without Explicit Syntax
proposals.motivation: Modern large language models (LLMs) process language sequentially, yet human understanding often involves hierarchical, compositional structure. While syntactic parsing provides one such hierarchy, it may not align with the semantic or task-specific structures most useful for model reasoning and knowledge manipulation, as highlighted by challenges in multi-hop question answering and knowledge editing (e.g., MQuAKE). This work investigates whether alternative, learned structures can improve model interpretability and robustness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that inducing latent tree structures over text based on semantic similarity and task-driven objectives—rather than pre-defined syntactic grammars—will yield representations that better support compositional reasoning and targeted knowledge updates in language models.
proposals.method: We will extend a Transformer-based encoder (e.g., T5 or a decoder-only LM) with a differentiable, latent tree induction module, such as a Gumbel-Softmax Tree-LSTM or a structured attention mechanism. This module will be trained with a multi-task objective combining: (1) a semantic similarity loss (using pre-2026 sentence embeddings) to encourage semantically related spans to merge, and (2) a downstream task reward (e.g., for multi-hop QA) optimized via policy gradient or Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to align tree formation with task utility. We will evaluate on edited knowledge probes (MQuAKE-style) and compositional generalization benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel method for learning latent, task-aware hierarchical structures in language, moving beyond syntax. It will provide new insights into how compositional representations can be induced in LLMs and offer a testbed for improving the precision and locality of model updates, advancing work on knowledge editing and model adaptation.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on syntactic parsing or static semantic role labeling, our method dynamically induces trees driven by a combination of generic semantic signals and specific task rewards, challenging the necessity of explicit syntactic formalism for building useful hierarchical representations in LLMs.

proposals.title: GNN-SYNTH: Syntactic Graph Neural Networks for Factual Consistency and Preference Tuning
proposals.motivation: Current large language models (LLMs) process text as token sequences, losing explicit syntactic structure which is crucial for precise meaning representation and multi-hop reasoning. This can lead to factual inconsistencies and difficulties in fine-grained control via methods like RLHF. Explicitly modeling language as a syntactic graph could provide a more structured, interpretable representation for knowledge editing and preference alignment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that representing sentences as syntactic dependency graphs and processing them with specialized Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) will improve a model's ability to maintain factual consistency during knowledge updates and enable more precise, structure-aware fine-tuning via Direct Preference Optimization (DPO).
proposals.method: We will develop a GNN-based encoder that operates on Universal Dependencies parse graphs, integrating it with a pre-trained LLM (e.g., T5 or LLaMA). For factual consistency, we will adapt knowledge editing benchmarks like MQuAKE, using the GNN to propagate updates through syntactic relations. For preference tuning, we will use the GNN's node representations to compute structure-aware rewards for DPO, building on meta-learning techniques for online adaptation to different syntactic schemas.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, structure-aware neural architecture for language processing that bridges symbolic syntactic information with deep learning. It will provide new evaluation paradigms for factual consistency in knowledge editing and a more granular method for aligning LLM outputs with human preferences based on syntactic structure.
proposals.novelty: While GNNs have been applied to semantic graphs (e.g., AMR), their systematic application to *syntactic* dependency trees for core LLM tasks like knowledge editing and DPO-based tuning is novel. This moves beyond using syntax as a passive preprocessing step to making it an active, learnable component of the model's reasoning process.

proposals.title: Learning Discriminative Non-Local Features for Robust and Efficient Knowledge Editing in Language Models
proposals.motivation: Current knowledge editing methods, like those evaluated in MQuAKE, struggle with multi-hop reasoning and can cause catastrophic forgetting or unintended side-effects. This limits the reliable, post-deployment adaptation of language models. A core challenge is the lack of mechanisms to automatically identify and modify the specific, often long-distance, internal features that discriminatively govern factual associations.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a structured, non-local Gibbs inference framework with gradient-based discriminative feature learning will enable precise, localized edits to a language model's knowledge, improving edit specificity, multi-hop consistency, and generalization while minimizing interference with unrelated knowledge.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage algorithm. First, we will use gradient-based saliency (inspired by work on fact localization) to automatically identify a sparse set of attention heads and feed-forward neurons that are discriminative for a target factual relation. Second, we will formulate knowledge editing as a constrained optimization within a non-local Gibbs sampling framework, where the sampling process is biased to update only the identified discriminative features, using a KL-divergence constraint akin to DPO's direct preference objective to ensure the edit generalizes without distorting the model's base behavior.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, more surgical paradigm for model editing that moves beyond direct parameter overwrites. It will provide a formal, inference-based method for learning and targeting the internal features responsible for specific knowledge, leading to improved metrics on benchmarks like MQuAKE for multi-hop consistency and edit locality.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of discriminative feature discovery—typically used for analysis—into an active inference-based editing procedure, creating a closed-loop system that learns *which* features to edit as part of the editing objective itself, a departure from static localization or global fine-tuning methods.

proposals.title: Inverse Constraint Discovery: Meta-Learning to Identify Task-Optimal Long-Distance Dependencies
proposals.motivation: Current methods for injecting constraints into language models, such as fine-tuning with RLHF or knowledge editing, rely on manually specified or heuristically generated constraints. This is inefficient and may miss the most impactful long-distance dependencies for a specific task and dataset. Automating the discovery of valuable constraints is a critical step towards more adaptive and sample-efficient model alignment and specialization.
proposals.hypothesis: The most valuable long-distance constraints for a given task can be automatically discovered by meta-learning a policy that maps from task and dataset characteristics to a distribution over constraint candidates, optimizing for downstream performance gain per constraint.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage meta-learning framework. First, we train a constraint proposer network via meta-gradient descent on a distribution of tasks, using performance of a base LM (fine-tuned with the proposed constraints via DPO or online adaptation) as the meta-reward. Second, for a new target task, we use the proposer to generate a ranked set of constraint candidates, which are validated using a small validation set and an efficient editing probe inspired by MQuAKE's multi-hop assessment. The final constraint set is integrated via fine-tuning or lightweight editing.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic, automated method for identifying task-critical constraints, moving beyond manual specification. It contributes a new benchmark for evaluating constraint discovery and a meta-learned proposer model that can accelerate adaptation of LMs to new domains with complex relational or factual requirements.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., MQuAKE, DPO) evaluates or enforces given constraints, we invert the problem to *discover* them. The approach uniquely combines meta-learning for constraint generation with efficient validation using knowledge-editing assessment techniques.

proposals.title: Constrained Generation for Faithful Data Augmentation: Using Information Extraction Templates to Guide Language Model Output
proposals.motivation: Information extraction (IE) systems require large, high-quality training data, which is expensive and time-consuming to create. While language models can generate text, their outputs often lack the precise factual structure needed for IE tasks. This work addresses the critical need for scalable, controllable generation of realistic text that reliably populates specific IE templates, enabling high-fidelity data augmentation and system testing.
proposals.hypothesis: A language model, fine-tuned via a constrained decoding objective derived from an IE schema, can generate fluent, naturalistic text that consistently satisfies the template's structural and factual constraints, thereby producing high-quality synthetic data for downstream IE models.
proposals.method: We will build on recent work in constrained decoding and Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). First, we will formalize IE templates (e.g., for relation or event extraction) as hard and soft constraints over the output space. We will then fine-tune a base LM (e.g., LLaMA or T5) using a two-stage process: 1) Supervised fine-tuning on existing IE-annotated corpora to learn the mapping from templates to text, and 2) DPO-based alignment to optimize for both fluency and constraint satisfaction, using a reward model that scores adherence to the template. Generation will be guided by a neuro-symbolic decoder that integrates the learned model with a finite-state automaton representing the template constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodology for generating semantically structured text, directly addressing data scarcity in IE. It will yield a new benchmark for evaluating the factual precision of constrained generation and demonstrate measurable improvements in the performance of IE models trained on the resulting synthetic data.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses constraints for controlled generation or edits models for factuality (MQuAKE), our approach uniquely inverts the IE process, using the extraction template as a direct, differentiable guide for text creation, combining DPO for alignment with symbolic constraints for guaranteed structure.

proposals.title: Coherence-Constrained RLHF for Factually Grounded Abstractive Summarization
proposals.motivation: Abstractive summarization models often produce outputs that are factually inconsistent with the source text or internally incoherent, limiting their reliability for real-world applications. While Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) has improved alignment, its rewards typically target overall preference, not explicitly decomposing and optimizing for the dual objectives of factual fidelity and narrative coherence. This gap necessitates a targeted reinforcement learning approach that directly shapes these critical summary qualities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a reinforcement learning framework using separate, learnable reward models for factual consistency and textual coherence—trained on targeted human feedback—will yield summarization models that significantly outperform those trained with monolithic preference models on both factual accuracy and readability metrics.
proposals.method: We will fine-tune a large language model (e.g., T5 or a decoder-only model) for summarization using a two-stage process. First, we will train two distinct reward models: one on human judgments of factuality against the source (inspired by factuality datasets) and another on judgments of local/global coherence. Second, we will apply Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) or online RL (PPO) with a composite reward function weighting these two signals. The training will leverage adversarial or perturbed summaries to strengthen robustness, and evaluation will use benchmarks like SummEval and the multi-hop factual consistency protocol from MQuAKE.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, modular framework for RLHF that disentangles and explicitly optimizes for core summary dimensions. It will yield a state-of-the-art summarization model with demonstrably higher factual reliability and coherence, accompanied by new diagnostic datasets for factuality and coherence rewards. The methodology will offer a blueprint for applying multi-objective RLHF to other generative tasks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in decomposing the RLHF reward for summarization into specialized, trainable models for factuality and coherence, moving beyond a single preference model. This approach directly addresses the core failure modes of summarization in a more controlled and interpretable manner than prior holistic RLHF applications.

proposals.title: Compositional Context-Aware Embeddings (CoCAE): Learning Dynamic Word Representations via Meta-Learning and Preference Optimization
proposals.motivation: Standard language models rely on fixed, context-independent word embeddings, which struggle with nuanced compositional semantics and dynamic meaning shifts in multi-hop reasoning. This limitation hinders precise knowledge editing and factual consistency, as seen in challenges like those addressed by MQuAKE. Enabling word vectors to adapt compositionally during training could lead to more flexible and accurate representations of meaning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that meta-learning a policy to dynamically adjust word vector parameters based on local compositional context during training will yield language models with improved compositional generalization and more robust, editable internal knowledge representations.
proposals.method: We will extend a transformer LM architecture by replacing static token embeddings with a small, meta-learned modulation network. This network will take the local syntactic and semantic context (e.g., from a shallow parse or neighboring embeddings) as input and produce a dynamic adjustment to the base embedding for each token instance during forward passes. The model will be trained using a two-stage process: first, meta-learning the modulation network for fast adaptation to new compositional patterns, followed by fine-tuning via Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) on datasets requiring multi-hop reasoning to align dynamic representations with factual preferences.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel architecture for dynamic word representations, moving beyond the fixed embedding paradigm. It will provide new insights into how compositional meaning can be encoded in LMs and offer a potential pathway for more effective knowledge editing, directly building on themes of fine-tuning and RLHF in Manning's recent work.
proposals.novelty: While contextual representations from models like BERT are dynamic per forward pass, our approach meta-learns to *fundamentally alter the training dynamics* of the embedding parameters themselves based on composition, which is distinct from standard contextualization. It combines meta-learning for online adaptation with preference optimization to steer this dynamic process, a novel synthesis.

proposals.title: Knowledge-Augmented Compositional Fine-Tuning: Integrating External Knowledge Bases for Robust Phrase Understanding
proposals.motivation: While large language models (LLMs) encode vast factual knowledge, their compositional reasoning—understanding novel combinations of known entities and relations—remains brittle, often failing on multi-hop queries or out-of-distribution phrases. This limits their reliability in applications requiring precise, knowledge-grounded language understanding. Recent work like MQuAKE highlights the difficulty of editing and maintaining factual knowledge in models, pointing to a need for architectures that can dynamically access and compose external knowledge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fine-tuning a pre-trained language model to dynamically retrieve and integrate information from a structured knowledge base (KB) during phrase processing will significantly improve its compositional generalization and factual accuracy on knowledge-intensive language understanding tasks.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage method. First, we adapt a dense retriever (e.g., DPR) to fetch relevant KB subgraphs (triples) for an input phrase. Second, we fine-tune a language model (e.g., T5 or an encoder-decoder) using a modified DPO objective, where preferences are defined by the correctness of the model's output given the retrieved knowledge versus without it. The model will be trained on datasets requiring compositional knowledge (e.g., MetaQA, compositional splits of OpenBookQA) to learn to attend to and reason over the provided KB context.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel fine-tuning framework that explicitly marries parametric knowledge in LLMs with non-parametric, updatable knowledge bases, improving robustness to knowledge updates and compositional queries. It will also provide new insights into how preference-based tuning can guide models to rely on external evidence, advancing methods for building more factual and interpretable NLP systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in combining knowledge retrieval with Direct Preference Optimization to train models to prefer knowledge-grounded compositions, moving beyond simple retrieval-augmented generation or static knowledge editing. This approach directly targets the compositional reasoning failure mode identified in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Semantic Structure Trees: A Treebank for Modeling Sarcasm, Intensity, and Modality
proposals.motivation: Current syntactic treebanks like the Stanford Dependencies have been instrumental for parsing and understanding grammatical structure, but they fail to capture crucial semantic and pragmatic phenomena that are essential for nuanced language understanding. Phenomena like sarcasm, intensity, and modality are central to communication, sentiment, and factuality, yet models lack fine-grained, structured annotations to learn their compositional nature. Addressing this gap is critical for improving model robustness in information retrieval, dialogue, and fact verification, especially as models are fine-tuned and aligned via methods like RLHF.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that creating structured, dependency-style treebanks for semantic phenomena will enable the training of more interpretable and accurate models for detecting and generating nuanced language. Specifically, we predict that models trained on these annotations will outperform those trained on flat labels on compositional generalization tasks involving these phenomena.
proposals.method: We will design novel annotation schemas extending the Stanford Dependencies framework to represent semantic relations for sarcasm (e.g., ironic contrast), intensity (e.g., degree modifiers), and modality (e.g., epistemic, deontic). Using a multi-stage process, we will first collect and annotate a high-quality corpus, leveraging expert linguists and validated crowdsourcing protocols. We will then develop a neural graph parser, building on recent advances in meta-learning for adaptation, to predict these semantic structures. The parser's performance will be evaluated on held-out test sets and through downstream tasks like fact editing (inspired by MQuAKE) and preference modeling.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute the first publicly available, large-scale treebanks for these semantic phenomena, providing a vital resource for the community. It will also deliver a novel parsing model capable of jointly predicting syntactic and semantic-pragmatic structure, enabling more fine-grained analysis and control of language model outputs, particularly in the context of knowledge editing and alignment.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has created datasets with flat labels for sentiment or sarcasm, no resource provides a fine-grained, compositional dependency structure for these phenomena, which is necessary for modeling their interaction with syntax and logic. The integration of this structured semantic parsing with modern techniques for model adaptation and editing represents a novel synthesis of linguistic formalism and deep learning.

proposals.title: Cross-Modal Attention for Compositional Factual Grounding in Language Models
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in RLHF and fine-tuning, large language models (LLMs) still struggle with compositional generalization and factual consistency, as highlighted by benchmarks like MQuAKE. Current attention mechanisms in transformers, while powerful, are not explicitly designed to weight and compose critical sub-phrases for generating multi-hop factual statements. Vision models have developed sophisticated attention mechanisms (e.g., spatial and channel attention in CNNs, or cross-attention in multimodal models) for dynamically weighting important visual features, which could offer a novel blueprint for linguistic composition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting structured visual attention mechanisms—specifically, mechanisms that perform explicit feature selection and cross-feature gating—into the language modeling architecture will enable more precise weighting of key words and sub-phrases during text generation, leading to improved performance on compositional reasoning and factual editing tasks.
proposals.method: We will design a novel 'Compositional Attention Layer' inspired by visual attention modules like Squeeze-and-Excitation networks and non-local blocks. This layer will be inserted into a pre-trained LLM (e.g., a T5 or GPT variant) and trained using a meta-learning online adaptation framework. The layer will compute a secondary attention score over token representations, conditioned on the ongoing compositional context, to explicitly up-weight factually critical sub-phrases. We will evaluate using the MQuAKE benchmark for multi-hop knowledge editing and employ Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to fine-tune the model's outputs for factual consistency, using the adapted attention scores as an interpretable signal for reward modeling.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a new, interpretable attention mechanism for LLMs that improves compositional factual grounding, 2) empirical evidence on the transferability of visual attention designs to linguistic tasks, and 3) an enhanced model for knowledge editing that addresses the limitations identified in pre-2026 studies. The resulting architecture will provide finer control over the information flow during generation.
proposals.novelty: While cross-attention is used in multimodal settings, the direct architectural adaptation of feature-weighting mechanisms from vision CNNs to core language model composition is unexplored. This approach moves beyond standard self-attention by adding a structured, context-aware feature gating step specifically optimized for factual sub-phrases, differing from prior work on general attention or RLHF.

proposals.title: Inverse Sentiment-to-Tree Generation: Probing Compositional Biases in Fine-Tuned Language Models
proposals.motivation: While language models fine-tuned with RLHF excel at generating text with target sentiments, the underlying compositional structures they favor remain a black box. Understanding this mapping from abstract affective goals to concrete syntactic-semantic forms is crucial for interpretability and for diagnosing models' potential biases, such as associating positive sentiment only with simple, short constructions. This work addresses a core gap in analyzing the 'how' of controlled generation beyond just the 'what'.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fine-tuned language models encode a non-uniform, learnable prior over plausible phrase structures for a given sentiment, and that this prior can be explicitly modeled and sampled from by inverting the typical text-to-sentiment pipeline.
proposals.method: We will train a conditional generative model (e.g., a Transformer decoder) to produce constituency or dependency tree representations given a continuous sentiment embedding. The model will be trained on pairs of (tree, sentiment score) derived by parsing sentences from a sentiment-annotated corpus and using a pre-existing sentiment classifier. We will employ Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) on tree outputs to refine the generator based on human or proxy preferences for structural plausibility, moving beyond simple reconstruction loss. The approach will be evaluated by generating trees for target sentiments and using an off-the-shelf LM to realize them into text, assessing both sentiment accuracy and syntactic naturalness.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel diagnostic tool for understanding the structural biases of sentiment-controlled LMs and introduce a new task of inverse conditional structure generation. It will also provide a methodology for probing the compositional knowledge acquired during fine-tuning, linking high-level directives to low-level syntactic choices.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the standard sentiment analysis pipeline to generate *structures* rather than surface strings, and in using preference optimization—typically applied to text—to refine abstract syntactic outputs, creating a direct interface between affective goals and compositional form.

proposals.title: Data Vaccines: Immunizing Language Models with Curated Domain Antigens for Targeted Robustness
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm in language model training prioritizes scale, using vast, unfiltered web corpora. However, this approach is inefficient and can lead to brittle, inconsistent performance on specialized domains, as seen in knowledge editing benchmarks like MQuAKE. A more targeted, data-efficient strategy is needed to build models that are robust and reliable for specific high-stakes applications, such as scientific reasoning or legal analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: A language model fine-tuned on a small, strategically curated dataset of domain-specific 'antigens'—core concepts, reasoning patterns, and adversarial edge cases—will achieve superior robustness and data efficiency within that domain compared to a model trained on orders of magnitude more general data or standard fine-tuning corpora.
proposals.method: We will develop a methodology for synthesizing 'data vaccines'. First, we will use a base model (e.g., LLaMA-2) and a meta-learning framework for online adaptation to identify knowledge gaps and failure modes within a target domain (e.g., multi-hop factual reasoning). Second, we will generate a curated dataset of synthetic examples that embody these core 'antigens', including counterfactuals and adversarial queries. Finally, we will 'immunize' the model using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) on this curated set, treating correct vs. incorrect reasoning on antigenic examples as preference pairs, rather than performing standard supervised fine-tuning.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for data-centric AI that prioritizes curation over scale, offering a path to more efficient and robust domain-specific models. It will yield a concrete methodology for 'data vaccine' creation and empirical evidence challenging the scale-equals-performance assumption in specialized contexts.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the standard scaling approach by arguing that a small, maximally informative dataset, constructed analogously to a vaccine, can outperform massive general data for domain-specific robustness. It uniquely combines insights from knowledge editing, preference optimization, and meta-learning for targeted dataset synthesis.

proposals.title: Benchmark-Driven Composition of Robust Model Collectives via Failure Decomposition
proposals.motivation: Current large language models exhibit brittle performance on complex, multi-faceted tasks despite their scale, as evidenced by benchmarks like MQuAKE for knowledge editing. Post-hoc robustness evaluations are insufficient for guiding architectural innovation. A systematic methodology is needed to decompose task failures and use that analysis to construct more reliable systems from heterogeneous components, moving beyond monolithic fine-tuning.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-model system, whose specialized components are explicitly selected or trained to compensate for weaknesses identified by a structured failure benchmark, will achieve greater robustness and efficiency on composite tasks than a single, homogeneously fine-tuned model of comparable total capacity.
proposals.method: First, we will develop a diagnostic benchmark suite that decomposes tasks (e.g., multi-hop QA) into failure modes (temporal, spatial, factual inconsistency). Second, we will use this suite to profile a zoo of smaller, specialized models (e.g., via fine-tuning or DPO). Third, we will design and train a lightweight routing model, inspired by meta-learning for online adaptation, to dynamically compose these specialists based on the input's predicted failure profile. The system will be optimized end-to-end using a robustness-aware reward model.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield: 1) a new benchmark for failure mode analysis in model collectives, 2) a principled framework for composing heterogeneous models, and 3) empirical evidence that such collectives outperform monolithic models on robustness metrics. It advances the field from single-model RLHF towards systematic, benchmark-driven system design.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on model ensembles or mixture-of-experts, our approach co-designs the evaluation benchmark and the system architecture, using granular failure analysis as the direct blueprint for model selection and interaction protocol design.

proposals.title: Adversarial Understanding Tests: Probing Causal Models and Semantic Grounding in Language Models
proposals.motivation: Large language models demonstrate impressive behavioral competence, but it remains unclear if this stems from genuine understanding or sophisticated pattern matching. This distinction is critical for trust and safety, especially as models are deployed in high-stakes domains requiring causal reasoning. Drawing an analogy from philosophical debates on consciousness, we propose a new evaluation paradigm to rigorously test for understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that current large language models, despite high performance on standard benchmarks, will fail systematic adversarial tests designed to reveal a lack of deep causal models and grounded semantic representations.
proposals.method: We will design a suite of 'adversarial understanding tests' inspired by philosophical arguments. This includes generating multi-hop counterfactual question chains (extending MQuAKE's approach), probing token-to-referent grounding in interactive environments, and evaluating consistency under novel compositional perturbations. We will apply these tests to models fine-tuned with DPO and those adapted via meta-learning, analyzing failure modes to isolate statistical correlation from causal reasoning.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, philosophically-grounded framework for evaluating understanding in AI, moving beyond task performance. It will produce a diagnostic benchmark that reveals specific weaknesses in model reasoning, guiding the development of more robust and interpretable systems. The findings will contribute to the debate on AI capabilities and limitations.
proposals.novelty: While existing work probes factual knowledge or robustness, our approach uniquely adapts the adversarial test structure from philosophy of mind to create evaluations specifically targeting causal understanding and semantic grounding, not just behavioral competence.

proposals.title: Cross-Lingual Generalization of Factual Knowledge Editing via Meta-Learning and DPO
proposals.motivation: Current knowledge editing techniques, like those in MQuAKE, are primarily validated on high-resource languages (e.g., English). Their efficacy for maintaining factual consistency across diverse and low-resource languages remains unknown. This is a critical gap, as deploying reliable, updatable models globally requires methods that work robustly across the world's linguistic diversity, not just a dominant few.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-learning framework for online adaptation, combined with Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) on multilingual preference data, can significantly improve the cross-lingual robustness and sample efficiency of factual knowledge editing in language models.
proposals.method: We will extend the MQuAKE evaluation framework to a curated set of typologically diverse high- and low-resource language pairs. Building on 'Meta-Learning Online Adaptation of Language Models', we will meta-train a model to quickly adapt its internal representations for factual updates. This adaptation will be guided by a DPO objective, using synthetically generated multilingual preference pairs (correct vs. incorrect post-edit statements) to steer the model's attention mechanisms toward cross-lingually consistent factual representations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic benchmark for cross-lingual knowledge editing and deliver a novel, sample-efficient method for performing reliable factual updates in multilingual settings. It will advance the goal of building language models that can be efficiently and accurately updated for global user bases.
proposals.novelty: While knowledge editing and multilingual training exist, their intersection is unexplored. The novel integration of meta-learning for rapid adaptation with a DPO-based objective specifically tuned for cross-lingual factual alignment presents a non-obvious and principled solution to this challenge.

proposals.title: Selective Lexical Infusion: Enhancing Factual Robustness in Unlexicalized Language Models via Meta-Learning and DPO
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) achieve high accuracy on many tasks but remain brittle to factual queries, especially multi-hop reasoning, as shown by benchmarks like MQuAKE. While purely unlexicalized models offer generalization, they can fail on specific factual knowledge. Conversely, full lexicalization or extensive fine-tuning risks overfitting and catastrophic forgetting. A method to inject minimal, precise lexical knowledge into a robust base model is needed to improve factual reliability without sacrificing generalization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a high-accuracy, unlexicalized base model can be made more factually robust for targeted knowledge domains through a meta-learning framework that selectively infuses lexical information, guided by Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to preserve overall coherence and avoid overfitting.
proposals.method: We will start with a high-performance unlexicalized base model (e.g., from meta-learning online adaptation work). Using a curated set of multi-hop factual queries (MQuAKE-style), we will identify critical knowledge gaps. For these gaps, we will create a sparse set of 'lexical anchors'—minimal parameter updates or adapter modules encoding the specific factual relations. A meta-learning outer loop will optimize the model to quickly integrate these anchors when relevant queries are detected, while an inner loop using DPO will ensure the model's responses to both lexicalized and unlexicalized inputs remain preferred, preventing drift and maintaining general task performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel hybrid architecture that balances the generalization of unlexicalized models with the precision of lexical knowledge. It will provide a new methodology for targeted, efficient model editing and factual updating, advancing beyond brute-force fine-tuning or retrieval-augmentation. The resulting model will demonstrate improved performance on multi-hop factual QA while maintaining strong performance on broader NLP benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the principled combination of meta-learning for selective adaptation with DPO for stability, creating a controlled mechanism for lexical infusion. This moves beyond simply adding retrieval or fine-tuning the entire model, instead learning *when* and *how* to activate specific lexical knowledge within a general-purpose base.

proposals.title: Unlexicalized, Distributed Constituency Parsing for Web-Scale Structured Knowledge Extraction
proposals.motivation: Modern language models (LMs) are trained on massive, unstructured text corpora, yet they often struggle with compositional reasoning and maintaining factual consistency, as highlighted by benchmarks like MQuAKE.
