A foundational, linguistically-grounded parse of this training data could provide a crucial structural scaffold to improve model reasoning and enable precise knowledge editing. However, scaling accurate syntactic parsing to web-scale corpora remains a significant computational and methodological challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an efficient, unlexicalized constituency parsing model, deployed on a distributed computing framework, can robustly parse petabyte-scale web text to produce a high-coverage syntactic database, and that this structured resource will significantly enhance the factual grounding and editing fidelity of large language models.
proposals.method: We will adapt and scale the unlexicalized, neural constituency parser from the Stanford NLP group (Kitaev & Klein, 2018) for distributed execution using Apache Spark or Ray. The core innovation involves a meta-learning strategy (inspired by 'Meta-Learning Online Adaptation of Language Models') to learn parsing adaptation policies that generalize across diverse web domains, minimizing the need for retraining. The parsed outputs will be indexed into a knowledge graph linking constituents to entity and relation extractions, creating a queryable 'syntactic knowledge base'.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first publicly available, web-scale syntactic parse tree database, a transformative resource for the community. It will provide a testbed for directly evaluating and improving the factual consistency of LMs via knowledge editing (e.g., applying DPO on parsed structures) and offer a new structural prior for training future models, moving beyond raw token sequences.
proposals.novelty: While distributed NLP is not new, applying a *linguistically precise, unlexicalized* parser at this scale to explicitly ground LM knowledge is novel. The integration of meta-learning for efficient domain adaptation of a parser within a massive distributed pipeline, aimed specifically at improving LM factual reasoning, is a unique synthesis of Manning group themes.

proposals.title: Semantic Parsing for Faithful Knowledge Editing: Translating Questions into Functional Programs
proposals.motivation: Recent work on knowledge editing, such as MQuAKE, highlights the challenge of updating factual knowledge in language models (LMs) consistently across multi-hop questions. A core difficulty is the ambiguity of natural language questions, which makes it hard to precisely target and modify the specific parametric knowledge the model uses. A more structured, executable representation of a question's intent could provide a reliable interface for editing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting semantic parsing models to translate natural language questions into a formal functional program representation will enable more precise, reliable, and composable edits to a language model's knowledge base, improving performance on multi-hop reasoning after editing.
proposals.method: We will fine-tune a pre-trained sequence-to-sequence model (e.g., T5) on a dataset of (question, functional program) pairs, where programs are constructed from a library of atomic knowledge operations (e.g., `get_property(entity, property)`). This parser will be integrated into an editing pipeline: for a given edit fact, we first parse the corresponding question to a program, use its structure to identify relevant model parameters, and apply a model editing algorithm (e.g., ROME or MEMIT). We will then use Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to align the model's outputs with the structured reasoning path, ensuring faithfulness to the edited program.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel pipeline that bridges high-precision semantic parsing with parametric knowledge editing, offering a more rigorous testbed for editing techniques. It will provide new benchmarks and metrics for evaluating the compositional consistency of edited models, moving beyond surface-level question answering.
proposals.novelty: While semantic parsing is well-studied for querying databases, its application as a precise interface for *editing* the internal knowledge of a language model, combined with alignment via DPO for reasoning faithfulness, is a novel direction. This moves beyond treating edits as textual prompts to treating them as operations on a model's latent functional graph.

proposals.title: Program-to-Question Generation: Training Language Models for Diverse, Compositional Question Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Current visual reasoning benchmarks (e.g., CLEVR) focus on training models to answer questions about scenes, creating a one-way evaluation of program execution. This limits our ability to audit and improve model understanding, as we cannot systematically generate the questions that probe specific compositional reasoning skills. An inverse model that generates valid, diverse questions from a scene and a target program would enable automated creation of diagnostic datasets and provide a novel lens for analyzing model capabilities and failures.
proposals.hypothesis: A language model, fine-tuned with a structured decoding objective and optimized for diversity via direct preference optimization (DPO) against a simplicity bias, can generate a diverse set of valid natural language questions that faithfully correspond to a given scene representation and target executable program.
proposals.method: We will build on encoder-decoder architectures (e.g., T5). The encoder takes a structured scene graph and a target program (e.g., a functional program sequence). The decoder is trained to generate a corresponding question. We will use a two-stage fine-tuning process: first, supervised fine-tuning on existing (scene, program, question) triplets from datasets like CLEVR. Second, we will apply Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) using a reward model that scores generated questions on validity (execution matches target) and lexical/syntactic diversity, creating a preference for diverse over trivial rephrasings. Online adaptation techniques will be explored to efficiently specialize the model to new visual domains.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel generative framework for creating diagnostic benchmarks to stress-test compositional reasoning in VLMs, 2) a methodology for using DPO to control stylistic attributes like diversity in conditional generation tasks beyond safety/alignment, and 3) a public model and dataset for inverse question generation to facilitate research in interpretable model evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work edits model knowledge (MQuAKE) or optimizes for human preferences (DPO), applying DPO to optimize for *diversity and validity* in a conditional *inverse* generation task—creating questions from programs—is unexplored. It inverts the standard paradigm from question answering to controlled question synthesis for evaluation.

proposals.title: Jointly Optimized Parsing and Factual Consistency via Constrained Fine-Tuning and DPO
proposals.motivation: Modern fine-tuned language models (LMs) often generate text that is locally fluent but globally inconsistent or factually incoherent, a problem highlighted by knowledge editing benchmarks like MQuAKE. Traditional statistical parsers excel at enforcing structural and semantic constraints but are not tightly integrated with LM generation. This gap suggests a need for models that can perform joint inference over linguistic structure and factual knowledge to improve coherence and reliability.
proposals.hypothesis: Tightly coupling a neural parser's constraints with an LM's generation process through joint optimization will produce text that is more structurally sound and factually consistent than models optimized for fluency or reward alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a joint inference framework where a transition-based neural dependency parser operates in tandem with a decoder-only LM. The parser will provide real-time syntactic and semantic constraints (e.g., argument structure, coreference links) during generation. We will formulate this as a constrained decoding problem and use Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to fine-tune the LM, where preferences are defined by joint scores combining parser-assigned constraint satisfaction and a factual reward model. This builds on recent work in online adaptation and RLHF, treating the parser as a source of structured preference signals.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a novel model architecture and training methodology for joint syntactic-semantic and factual inference. It will provide new benchmarks for evaluating the structural consistency of LM outputs and demonstrate how classical NLP components can guide and stabilize modern fine-tuning techniques like DPO.
proposals.novelty: While parsing has been used for analysis, its tight integration as a constraint provider during LM fine-tuning and generation, particularly via DPO, is unexplored. This moves beyond using parsers as post-hoc analyzers to making them core, optimizing components of the text generation process.

proposals.title: Globally Normalized Fine-Tuning: Integrating Conditional Random Fields for Consistent Sequence Tagging in Language Models
proposals.motivation: Modern large language models (LLMs) fine-tuned with RLHF or DPO excel at instruction following but can exhibit inconsistencies in structured prediction tasks like fact extraction or named entity recognition, where local token-level decisions must respect global constraints. This is a critical issue for applications requiring reliable information extraction, as highlighted by benchmarks like MQuAKE. Pre-2026 sequence tagging with LLMs often relies on autoregressive or locally normalized outputs, which lack explicit mechanisms for enforcing label dependencies across a sequence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a Conditional Random Field (CRF) layer as the output head of a fine-tuned LLM will improve consistency and accuracy in sequence tagging tasks by enabling globally normalized, joint inference over output labels, effectively marrying the semantic knowledge of LLMs with the structured prediction strengths of graphical models.
proposals.method: We will adapt a pre-trained language model (e.g., LLaMA or T5) for sequence tagging. The final transformer layer's hidden states will be used as emission scores for a linear-chain CRF. The model will be fine-tuned end-to-end using the negative log-likelihood of the correct label sequence under the CRF, contrasting with standard cross-entropy loss. We will evaluate this CRF-LM hybrid on multi-hop question answering (MQuAKE-style) and factual slot-filling tasks, comparing it against baseline fine-tuning and prompt-based methods, measuring both accuracy and label sequence consistency.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for injecting explicit structural biases into fine-tuned LLMs for sequence labeling, potentially leading to more reliable information extraction. It will yield empirical evidence on the benefits of global normalization versus standard local prediction in the context of modern LLMs, contributing to the literature on robust knowledge editing and model adaptation.
proposals.novelty: While CRFs are classic, their integration into the fine-tuning and adaptation pipeline of very large language models (post-RLHF/DPO) for complex, knowledge-intensive tagging is unexplored. This approach is distinct from simply using a CRF on static embeddings, as it jointly learns the deep feature representation and the structured output layer.

proposals.title: Efficient Cross-Lingual Adaptation via Meta-Learned Feature Selection and Direct Preference Optimization for Low-Resource Languages
proposals.motivation: Current large language models (LLMs) are heavily biased towards high-resource languages, leaving morphologically rich and low-resource languages underserved due to a lack of annotated data. This gap limits the global applicability and fairness of NLP technologies. Adapting existing models efficiently for these languages is a critical, unsolved challenge in pre-2026 NLP.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-learning framework for adaptive feature selection, combined with Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) on minimal human feedback, can enable a pre-trained multilingual model to achieve strong performance on low-resource languages with far less task-specific annotated data than current fine-tuning methods require.
proposals.method: We will extend the meta-learning online adaptation framework to learn a language-specific feature selector that identifies and activates the most relevant subnetwork of a base multilingual model (e.g., mT5) for a given low-resource language. This meta-learner will be trained on a diverse set of medium-resource languages. Subsequently, we will use Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) with a small set of human preferences (e.g., on fluency or factual correctness, inspired by MQuAKE's focus on facts) to align the model's outputs for the target language, avoiding the need for large-scale supervised fine-tuning or complex reward model training.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, data-efficient pipeline for adapting LLMs to low-resource languages. It will contribute a new benchmark for evaluating such adaptations on morphologically rich languages and advance the theoretical understanding of which model features transfer cross-lingually. The resulting method will be a step towards democratizing access to high-quality NLP.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of meta-learned feature selection for cross-lingual transfer with the sample-efficient alignment of DPO, specifically targeting the dual challenges of morphological complexity and data scarcity. This combined approach to architectural and behavioral adaptation for low-resource settings is unexplored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Efficient Web-Scale Factual Refinement via Approximate Feature-Based DPO
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) trained on web-scale corpora contain vast but noisy and often contradictory factual knowledge. While methods like Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and knowledge editing (e.g., MQuAKE) can refine this knowledge, applying them directly to billions of tokens is computationally prohibitive. An efficient, scalable method is needed to align models with high-quality factual information across the entire training corpus.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a lightweight, feature-based approximation of the DPO loss, applied selectively to datapoints identified by a fast factual inconsistency detector, can efficiently steer a model's internal representations toward factual consistency at web-scale without full fine-tuning.
proposals.method: We will first train a small, efficient 'fact-sense' classifier on contrastive examples to detect tokens or spans likely to contain factual errors in a streaming corpus. For these high-error-probability spans, we will compute a feature-based DPO loss using a frozen, smaller reward model distilled from the main LLM, targeting only the activations of key factual knowledge layers (inspired by meta-learning for online adaptation). This will involve developing a gating mechanism and a sparse, block-wise backpropagation strategy to update only a subset of parameters per batch, enabling efficient training over billions of words.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical algorithm for web-scale knowledge alignment, a benchmark for evaluating factual consistency improvements on massive text, and new insights into which model features are most responsive to factual refinement. It bridges scalable RLHF approximations with targeted knowledge editing.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in combining a fast pre-filter for factual errors with a feature-targeted, approximate DPO update, moving beyond full-model fine-tuning or small-scale, post-hoc editing to enable efficient, corpus-wide factual calibration.

proposals.title: Controlled Text Generation via Latent Tag Guidance: A Distributional Alignment Approach
proposals.motivation: Current controlled text generation often relies on fine-tuning or external discriminators, which is computationally expensive and can degrade a model's general capabilities. A method to steer generation using lightweight, interpretable tag sequences—without full retraining—would enable more efficient and precise content control. This addresses a core challenge in making large language models more adaptable and reliable for specific information-centric tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a language model's internal probability distributions can be directly aligned with a sequence of semantic tags, enabling the generation of fluent, factually consistent text that adheres to the specified tag constraints without task-specific fine-tuning.
proposals.method: We will develop a training-free inference-time method. Given a tag sequence (e.g., [PERSON, ACTION, LOCATION]), we will compute a guidance signal by comparing the model's next-token distributions against distributions from a 'tag-conditioned' prior, derived from a small auxiliary model or curated prompts. This signal will be used to steer the base model's sampling, akin to a lightweight, gradient-free version of contrastive decoding. We will integrate techniques from online adaptation and direct preference optimization to refine the alignment between tag semantics and generated text, ensuring factual consistency as evaluated by methods like those in MQuAKE.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, efficient paradigm for controlled generation that preserves a model's base knowledge. It will provide a formal framework for tag-based guidance as a distributional alignment problem. The resulting method will be empirically validated on tasks requiring multi-hop factual consistency and stylistic control, advancing the field of post-hoc model steering.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prompt engineering or fine-tuning, our approach manipulates the model's internal distributions in real-time to follow a tag map, blending ideas from controlled generation, knowledge editing, and online adaptation in a novel, training-light framework.

proposals.title: Grounded NLI via Multi-Modal Fact Verification and Dynamic Preference Optimization
proposals.motivation: Current Natural Language Inference (NLI) datasets often rely on image captions as grounding, which introduces bias towards descriptive, often superficial, visual relations. This limits models' ability to perform robust, fact-based reasoning about the world. As language models grow more capable, there is a pressing need for NLI benchmarks that test deeper factual and causal understanding grounded in diverse, verifiable sources beyond static captions.
proposals.hypothesis: NLI data constructed by grounding hypotheses in a dynamic, multi-source knowledge base (e.g., knowledge graphs, verified web text, structured tables) will produce more challenging and reliable benchmarks, leading to models with stronger factual consistency and multi-hop reasoning abilities than those trained on caption-grounded data alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a pipeline to generate premise-hypothesis pairs by sampling facts from a curated knowledge graph (e.g., Wikidata) and web-scale corpora, using T5 or GPT-3 to verbalize them. To ensure quality, we will apply a filtering model trained via Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) on human judgments of logical validity and factual relevance, building on DPO's efficiency over RLHF. We will then fine-tune and evaluate LMs (e.g., LLaMA, T5) on this new dataset, using meta-learning techniques for online adaptation to assess robustness to unseen factual relations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, publicly available NLI dataset that reduces caption-induced bias and emphasizes factual and causal reasoning. It will provide a framework for using DPO and knowledge editing methods (inspired by MQuAKE evaluations) to create high-quality, grounded textual entailment data, advancing the evaluation of factual consistency in language models.
proposals.novelty: It moves beyond the dominant paradigm of image-caption grounding for NLI, instead grounding reasoning in structured, multi-source factual knowledge and using DPO for scalable data curation, creating a benchmark that directly tests a model's integration of external knowledge for inference.

proposals.title: Fact-Aware Meta-Learning for Robust and Compositional Natural Language Inference
proposals.motivation: Despite the strong performance of large language models (LLMs) on Natural Language Inference (NLI), they remain brittle to compositional reasoning and factual inconsistencies, as shown by benchmarks like MQuAKE. This limits their reliability in real-world applications requiring multi-step inference grounded in accurate world knowledge. Addressing this gap is crucial for developing models that truly understand language.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model architecture combining meta-learning for rapid adaptation with a structured, editable fact memory will significantly improve robustness on compositional NLI tasks requiring factual knowledge, outperforming standard fine-tuning and prompting approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a model based on a T5 or encoder-decoder Transformer, augmented with a differentiable, key-value memory network storing factual knowledge tuples. Inspired by meta-learning for online adaptation, we will train the model using MAML to quickly adapt its inference strategy and memory access patterns to new compositional question types. Training will leverage datasets like SNLI, MultiNLI, and constructed adversarial sets from MQuAKE, using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to refine outputs towards logically sound and factually consistent reasoning chains.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel NLI architecture integrating meta-learning with an editable fact memory, 2) a new benchmark for evaluating compositional and factual robustness in NLI, and 3) empirical evidence that such hybrid models reduce hallucination and improve systematic generalization compared to standard LLMs.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic integration of meta-learning for inference strategy adaptation with a dynamically editable fact store, moving beyond static fine-tuning or retrieval-augmentation to create models that can learn *how* to reason with facts in novel compositional settings.

proposals.title: Learning Factually Grounded Semantic Representations via Multi-Hop Entailment and Direct Preference Optimization
proposals.motivation: Current large language models (LLMs) encode semantic knowledge but often generate inconsistent or non-factual statements, especially under multi-hop reasoning. The SNLI corpus, with its large-scale, human-annotated entailment pairs, provides a structured testbed for probing and improving the coherence and factual grounding of semantic representations. This work addresses the core challenge of making model-internal representations more reliably align with verifiable world knowledge and logical inference chains.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fine-tuning language models on multi-hop entailment tasks derived from SNLI, guided by a Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) objective that rewards factually consistent reasoning chains, will yield internal representations that are more robust, generalizable, and factually accurate than those from standard supervised fine-tuning or RLHF.
proposals.method: We will construct a multi-hop dataset by chaining SNLI premises and hypotheses to form complex inference graphs. A base LM (e.g., LLaMA-2) will be meta-learned to rapidly adapt to new inference patterns. The core training will use DPO, where the reward model is implicitly defined by preference pairs: chains leading to factually correct entailment vs. those containing logical or factual errors (seeded using counterfactuals from knowledge editing benchmarks like MQuAKE). We will analyze the resulting representations via probing and their performance on downstream tasks requiring semantic understanding.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new methodology for learning semantically robust representations without explicit reward model training, and a benchmark for evaluating the factual consistency of representations in multi-hop inference. It will advance understanding of how preference-based tuning can shape not just outputs but the internal semantic structure of LMs.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in combining DPO—typically used for aligning model outputs with human preferences—with the structured semantic framework of SNLI to directly optimize for factual consistency in internal representations, a goal distinct from standard textual alignment. This approach moves beyond using SNLI merely as a classification task.

proposals.title: MegaNLI: Scaling Natural Language Inference to 10 Million Pairs for Robust Preference Learning and Factual Alignment
proposals.motivation: Large-scale Natural Language Inference (NLI) datasets like SNLI (570K pairs) have been foundational for training models to understand semantic relationships, but current models, especially those fine-tuned with RLHF or DPO for alignment, require orders of magnitude more diverse reasoning data. The success of methods like DPO, which implicitly uses a reward model derived from preference data, hinges on the model's underlying ability to make fine-grained, factual, and multi-hop inferences about textual statements, a capability that existing NLI corpora are insufficient to fully instill.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that creating a massively scaled NLI dataset (an order of magnitude larger than current resources) will provide a critical, high-quality knowledge substrate that significantly improves the robustness of language models in downstream tasks requiring factual reasoning, preference optimization, and online adaptation, as measured by benchmarks like MQuAKE.
proposals.method: We will scale data collection by leveraging a hybrid human-in-the-loop and LLM-augmented pipeline. First, we will use large language models (e.g., GPT-4, Claude 2) to generate diverse premise-hypothesis candidate pairs across a broad knowledge spectrum, guided by structured knowledge bases (e.g., Wikidata) to ensure factual grounding. These candidates will then be filtered and validated through a large-scale, gamified crowdsourcing platform to obtain high-quality human judgments for entailment, contradiction, and neutrality. We will specifically engineer prompts to generate multi-hop reasoning chains and counterfactual scenarios, creating a dataset rich in the complex inference patterns needed for robust model alignment.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the largest publicly available high-quality NLI dataset, designed explicitly for improving factual reasoning and alignment. We will demonstrate its utility by fine-tuning and applying DPO on models pre-trained on MegaNLI, showing measurable gains in knowledge editing robustness (evaluated via MQuAKE) and in the sample efficiency of online adaptation methods, providing a new standard resource for data-hungry alignment techniques.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has created NLI datasets, the proposed scale (targeting 10M pairs) and explicit focus on generating multi-hop, factually-grounded inference pairs for direct application to RLHF/DPO and knowledge editing benchmarks represent a novel, targeted effort to bridge scalable data creation with the specific needs of modern alignment algorithms.

proposals.title: Relational Cross-Modal Transfer: Enhancing Natural Language Inference with Scene Graph Architectures
proposals.motivation: Current large language models (LLMs) still struggle with robust multi-hop and compositional reasoning, as evidenced by benchmarks like MQuAKE. Meanwhile, computer vision has developed sophisticated architectures, such as scene graph networks and relation transformers, that explicitly model structured relationships between visual entities. Transferring these explicit relational inductive biases to the textual domain could address core weaknesses in semantic understanding for tasks like Natural Language Inference (NLI).
proposals.hypothesis: Explicit relational modeling architectures, proven effective for visual scene understanding, can be adapted to capture fine-grained semantic dependencies between sentences, leading to more robust and interpretable reasoning in NLI tasks, particularly for complex, multi-hop examples.
proposals.method: We will adapt a scene graph transformer or Graph Attention Network (GAN) architecture, pre-trained on visual relationship datasets like Visual Genome, to operate on textual graphs. Premise and hypothesis sentences will be parsed into semantic graphs using dependency parsers and open information extraction tools. The visual relation encoder will be fine-tuned on NLI datasets (e.g., SNLI, SciTail, and challenging subsets of ANLI) using a contrastive or Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) objective to align the learned relational representations with entailment judgments, building on recent work in efficient fine-tuning and online adaptation.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel, structured approach to semantic relation modeling for NLI, potentially improving performance on complex reasoning cases. It will yield a more interpretable model where reasoning traces can be visualized via the learned relational graphs, and contribute to the broader agenda of cross-modal transfer of inductive biases.
proposals.novelty: While cross-modal transfer is common, directly repurposing core architectural components from visual relation modeling for deep semantic textual reasoning is unexplored. This moves beyond simple feature alignment to transplanting structural inductive biases designed for relational reasoning.

proposals.title: Semantic Space Alignment: A Metric Learning Framework for Natural Language Inference
proposals.motivation: Current Natural Language Inference (NLI) models, while powerful, often function as black-box classifiers, lacking an explicit, structured representation of semantic relationships. This limits interpretability and robustness, particularly for compositional or multi-hop reasoning tasks as highlighted by recent work on knowledge editing. A geometric, distance-based representation of entailment and contradiction could provide a more fundamental and manipulable model of semantic meaning, aligning with the field's push towards more controllable and factually consistent language models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that metric learning can construct a continuous semantic vector space where the distance between sentence representations directly encodes their logical relationship, such that entailment corresponds to minimal distance and contradiction corresponds to maximal separation or a distinct directional offset.
proposals.method: We will adapt contrastive and triplet loss frameworks from metric learning to NLI. Given a premise sentence `p`, an entailing hypothesis `h_e`, and a contradicting hypothesis `h_c`, we will train a transformer encoder (e.g., a T5 or DeBERTa variant) to produce normalized embeddings. The training objective will enforce `d(p, h_e) < d(p, h_n) < d(p, h_c)` for all triples, where `d` is a distance metric like cosine distance. We will experiment with structured losses like angular margin loss and integrate techniques from meta-learning for rapid online adaptation to new domains, evaluating on datasets like SNLI, MultiNLI, and stress tests for systematic generalization.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, geometrically interpretable model for NLI that moves beyond classification scores to a structured semantic space. It will provide new tools for analyzing model reasoning and potential applications in knowledge editing (e.g., precisely moving concept representations) and preference optimization (e.g., using semantic distances as intrinsic rewards for RLHF).
proposals.novelty: While metric learning is established in vision and retrieval, its direct application to learn a *unified* space encoding discrete logical NLI relations is novel. This approach departs from standard cross-entropy classification, explicitly optimizing for the geometric structure of semantic relationships.

proposals.title: Phrase-Level Natural Language Inference: A Fine-Grained Benchmark for Lexical and Compositional Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Current Natural Language Inference (NLI) benchmarks operate at the sentence or paragraph level, evaluating holistic understanding but obscuring fine-grained reasoning failures. As language models are increasingly fine-tuned with RLHF and DPO for factual accuracy and coherence, we lack diagnostic tools to assess their understanding of sub-sentential units—the very building blocks of meaning. This gap limits our ability to precisely edit model knowledge (as in MQuAKE) or adapt models online, as errors in lexical or phrasal semantics can propagate to larger-scale mistakes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying the NLI framework to word- and phrase-level pairs will reveal significant, systematic failures in modern language models' lexical and compositional reasoning, failures that are not captured by standard sentence-level evaluation.
proposals.method: We will construct a new benchmark, PhraseNLI, by extracting and manually annotating phrase-premise pairs from existing NLI corpora and lexical resources (e.g., WordNet, FrameNet) with entailment, contradiction, and neutral labels. We will evaluate a range of models, including those fine-tuned via DPO and subjected to knowledge editing, using both zero-shot prompting and a contrastive fine-tuning approach inspired by meta-learning for rapid adaptation. Analysis will involve probing the models' internal representations to localize the source of phrasal inference errors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale, controlled benchmark for sub-sentential NLI, enabling precise diagnosis of lexical and compositional knowledge in LMs. It will create new evaluation protocols for knowledge editing and preference optimization methods, showing whether improvements at the sentence level truly reflect better understanding of constituent meanings.
proposals.novelty: While NLI is a mature field, its systematic application to sub-sentential units is novel, shifting the focus from broad textual understanding to the fine-grained semantic relationships that underpin it. This directly connects the methodological rigor of NLI to the granular knowledge editing and adaptation problems central to recent LM research.

proposals.title: Scaling and Probing the Limits of Global Vectors: A Study of Massive Corpora and Ultra-High Dimensionality
proposals.motivation: Despite the dominance of contextual language models, static word embeddings like GloVe remain foundational for their interpretability, efficiency, and use in resource-constrained or specialized applications. However, their performance has been evaluated on relatively modest corpora (e.g., Common Crawl) and dimensionalities (typically ≤300). It is unknown if scaling these two axes with modern computational resources yields qualitatively better representations, particularly for capturing nuanced semantic and factual knowledge, a core challenge in recent work on model editing and factual recall.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training GloVe on a truly massive, multi-domain corpus (e.g., a filtered petabyte-scale web dump) and scaling vector dimensions to several thousand will yield embeddings that significantly outperform standard baselines on fine-grained semantic similarity, multi-hop relational reasoning, and factual knowledge probing tasks.
proposals.method: We will implement a highly optimized, distributed GloVe training pipeline to handle petabyte-scale text. We will systematically sweep over corpus size (from 10B to 1T+ tokens) and dimensionality (300 to 10,000). Performance will be evaluated on standard benchmarks (e.g., SimLex, WordSim) and, crucially, on novel multi-hop factual inference tasks inspired by MQuAKE, probing the model's ability to compose relational knowledge. We will analyze the spectral properties and intrinsic dimensionality of the resulting vector spaces to understand the gains.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish empirical scaling laws for classic embedding methods in the modern data regime, providing a robust baseline for knowledge-intensive NLP. It will contribute a new, large-scale benchmark dataset for static embedding evaluation focused on factual knowledge chains. The resulting ultra-high-dimensional vectors could serve as efficient, static knowledge bases for lightweight model augmentation.
proposals.novelty: While scaling is a known principle, a systematic investigation of corpus and dimensional scaling for count-based models in the post-LLM era, with explicit evaluation on complex factual knowledge tasks, is novel. This bridges the classic embedding literature with contemporary concerns about factual reliability and efficient knowledge representation.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: generate questions from programs

ideas.title: Architecturally Diverse Preference-Optimized Ensembles (ADPOE)
ideas.description: This research proposes training an ecosystem of smaller, architecturally diverse language models—such as SSMs, RNNs, or hybrid models—using a shared DPO loss framework. Instead of applying DPO to homogenize their outputs, the training is designed to encourage specialization and complementary behavior, where different architectures learn to excel at distinct aspects of human preference (e.g., factual accuracy, creativity, safety, or coherence). The ensemble then uses a learned routing mechanism to dynamically combine these specialized outputs, creating a system that is more robust, efficient, and capable than a single large model, while also advancing our understanding of how alignment techniques generalize across neural architectures.

ideas.title: Domain-Specific Immunization: Curated Data as a Targeted Vaccine for AI Models
ideas.description: This idea inverts the assumption that broad, general-purpose training data is optimal by proposing that specialized, curated data can outperform it for specific domains. The analogy is drawn from Immunology/Vaccinology, where targeted vaccines (using curated antigens) are more effective than general immune boosters for preventing specific diseases. The analogous solution involves training the immune system with a precise, weakened version of a threat. Adapted to AI research, the solution is to develop a methodology for creating 'data vaccines'—carefully curated, domain-specific datasets designed to 'immunize' a model. This process would involve identifying and synthesizing the core 'antigens' (key concepts, reasoning patterns, and edge cases) of a target domain, then using this curated corpus for focused training. The expected outcome is that models trained with these data vaccines would achieve higher performance, data efficiency, and robustness within their specialized domain than models trained on orders of magnitude more general web data, challenging the prevailing scale-equals-performance paradigm.

ideas.title: Applying the Turing Test for Consciousness to AI: Differentiating Statistical Competence from Causal Understanding
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that AI model capabilities imply genuine understanding. By drawing an analogy from the Philosophy of Mind, specifically the debates surrounding the Turing Test and Searle's Chinese Room argument, we adapt their solution framework. The analogous field solved the problem of distinguishing behavioral competence from internal understanding by designing adversarial philosophical tests aimed at revealing a lack of semantic grounding or causal reasoning. Adapted to AI research, this involves creating novel evaluation suites—'adversarial understanding tests'—that probe whether a model's successful performance stems from deep causal models and grounded semantics or from sophisticated pattern matching of statistical correlations. These tests would present counterfactuals, require explanations of token-to-referent relationships, or evaluate consistency under novel compositional reasoning, providing evidence to separate statistical competence from genuine understanding.

ideas.title: Benchmarking and Designing for Robustness in Heterogeneous Multi-Model Systems
ideas.description: This research direction proposes to co-design new evaluation suites and heterogeneous multi-model architectures. Instead of applying robustness benchmarks to a single model post-hoc, we develop benchmarks that systematically map the failure landscape of tasks—identifying specific weaknesses like spatial reasoning, temporal coherence, or adversarial prompts. These benchmarks then inform the assembly of a collaborative ecosystem of smaller, specialized models, where each component is selected or trained to compensate for the failures of others. The ultimate goal is to create a methodology where benchmark-driven failure analysis directly guides the architectural composition and interaction protocols of model collectives, yielding systems that are more robust, efficient, and interpretable than any single foundation model.

ideas.title: Architecture-Aware Preference Optimization: Deriving Stable Objectives for Non-Transformer Language Models
ideas.description: This research systematically derives stable preference optimization methods for non-transformer language models, rather than directly applying the transformer-centric DPO loss. It first analyzes the training dynamics and output distribution properties of target architectures (e.g., SSMs, recurrent networks) to identify incompatibilities with the standard DPO formulation. The core of the approach is to reformulate the preference optimization objective from first principles, ensuring alignment with each architecture's specific parameterization and sequence modeling mechanism. The final system provides validated, architecture-specific training procedures that enable effective and stable alignment for diverse model families.

ideas.title: RankNet-DPO: Adapting Learning-to-Rank Gradient Methods for Nuanced Preference Optimization
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea to challenge DPO's binary preference assumption by drawing an analogy from Information Retrieval, specifically the Learning-to-Rank field. The analogous problem is learning a ranking function from pairwise document preferences, solved by algorithms like RankNet which use a differentiable, probabilistic cost function based on score differences. The adapted idea, RankNet-DPO, modifies the DPO objective to accept nuanced feedback like rankings or continuous scores. It replaces the binary logistic loss with a loss function where the target probability is not fixed at 1/0 but is a smooth function of the provided preference strength (e.g., a score delta or a ranking percentile). This allows the gradient updates to the language model policy to be proportional to the confidence or intensity of the human preference, enabling learning from more granular, non-binary feedback signals.

ideas.title: Robust Preference Optimization as a Probe for Genuine Reasoning
ideas.description: This research direction proposes using robust optimization techniques not merely to improve DPO's performance, but as an active probe for understanding. The core hypothesis is that a model relying on shallow statistical correlations will exhibit a sharp, predictable drop in policy performance when its preference labels are adversarially perturbed within a robust optimization framework. In contrast, a model developing genuine reasoning structures should demonstrate greater robustness, maintaining more stable performance across these perturbed preference distributions. By systematically analyzing the relationship between adversarial robustness in the preference objective and out-of-distribution generalization on reasoning benchmarks, this approach aims to develop a new metric for 'understanding' that is grounded in a model's resilience to optimization noise.

ideas.title: Robust Specialization via Dynamic Gating Between General Foundation and Curated Expert Models
ideas.description: This research investigates a hybrid architecture to achieve robust domain-specific performance without the brittleness of pure curated training. The system pairs a general-purpose foundation model with a deeply trained specialist model on high-quality curated data. A trainable gating network, exposed to a validation set containing domain edges and counterfactuals, dynamically routes inputs, blending the generalist's breadth with the specialist's depth. This approach aims to outperform both broad and purely specialized models by ensuring high in-domain accuracy while maintaining graceful degradation and adaptability on novel or shifting inputs.

ideas.title: Operationalizing Robust Reasoning: A Counterfactual Test Framework for Model Generalization
ideas.description: This research constructs a controlled synthetic domain with a transparent, programmatically defined causal logic to serve as a testbed. Models are trained on a standard corpus of this domain and then subjected to a battery of tests requiring generalization, including interventions on latent variables, novel combinations of known rules, and queries about necessary and sufficient conditions. The core metric is not a binary 'understanding' score but a detailed failure profile, identifying whether model performance degrades in ways predictable for a purely correlational learner versus a system that has constructed an internal model of the domain's underlying mechanics.

ideas.title: Robust Preference Learning with Distributional Feedback
ideas.description: This research direction develops a Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) variant that learns from distributional or ranked feedback, such as a full ranking of multiple responses or a confidence score for a preference, rather than a single binary label. It incorporates robust optimization principles by treating the observed nuanced feedback as a potentially noisy realization of an underlying true preference distribution, and it optimizes for worst-case performance within an uncertainty set around this observed data. This approach is particularly powerful for learning from crowdsourced or inherently subjective human feedback, where individual rankings or scores may be inconsistent. The synergy enables more data-efficient and reliable alignment by extracting more information per data point while being resilient to the noise that richer feedback mechanisms inevitably introduce.

ideas.title: Architecture-Agnostic Preference Optimization via State-Value Alignment
ideas.description: This idea proposes solving the challenge of applying Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to non-transformer architectures by drawing an analogy from computational neuroscience. In that field, to align complex, fixed biological networks with a goal, a separate critic network is trained to evaluate states and guide plasticity. Adapted here, we introduce a trainable 'preference projector' module. This module interfaces with the hidden states of any language model architecture (e.g., Mamba, RWKV, or classic RNNs). The standard DPO loss is computed on the projector's outputs, and gradients flow back through the projector to update both itself and, crucially, the primary model's pre-projection states. This creates an architecture-agnostic pathway for DPO, enabling preference alignment for models where the standard DPO formulation is mathematically or computationally problematic.

ideas.title: Meta-Learning for Dynamic Preference Alignment with Automated Critic Ensembles
ideas.description: This research direction meta-trains a model using a variant of DPO where the preference pairs are generated not by static human judgments, but by a dynamic, learned critic model or an automated system that evaluates outcomes. The meta-learning objective is to quickly adapt the policy to be aligned with whatever preference function the current critic embodies. This enables the system to personalize to individual users by rapidly tuning to their unique critic (which models their latent preferences) and to improve over time as the critic itself is updated based on user interaction feedback, creating a synergistic cycle of policy adaptation and preference discovery.

ideas.title: Massive-Scale Multi-Model Preference Optimization
ideas.description: This research direction develops a scalable system for Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) that can ingest millions of preference pairs from heterogeneous sources, each potentially governed by different human choice behaviors. Instead of forcing all data to conform to the standard Bradley-Terry model, the system learns to invert or apply alternative choice models from decision theory—such as Plackett-Luce, probit, or context-dependent models—based on the characteristics of each data subset or task. This allows for more accurate and robust alignment across diverse domains, from creative writing to technical code, by fitting the preference model to the data's inherent structure rather than the reverse. The core challenge involves creating efficient, modular frameworks for model selection, aggregation, and gradient computation across this massive, multi-model preference landscape.

ideas.title: Robust Multi-Source Preference Alignment via Modular DPO and Conflict Resolution
ideas.description: This research develops a system for scaling Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) that is robust to contradictions inherent in massive, diverse preference datasets. Instead of applying a single DPO loss across all data, it partitions preferences by source and semantic domain, training modular, composable policy components for each coherent cluster. A central conflict resolution module dynamically arbitrates between these components during training and inference, using techniques like uncertainty weighting or constrained optimization to prevent policy collapse. The result is a single model that can integrate millions of preferences without degenerating, maintaining performance across diverse tasks and aligning safely even with conflicting human feedback.

ideas.title: A Theory of Emergent Preference Adaptation in Meta-Learned Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research synthesizes scaling theory with meta-learning for alignment to develop a predictive framework for emergent preference adaptation. It investigates how the sample efficiency and robustness of a model's adaptation to new human preferences via Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) change as a function of both base model scale and the scale of the meta-training distribution over diverse preference sets. The goal is to derive scaling laws that predict thresholds where qualitatively new adaptation capabilities, such as one-shot preference inference or robust out-of-distribution alignment, emerge. This provides a principled path to build foundation models whose core competency is not just following instructions, but predictably and efficiently learning *how* to align with any individual.

ideas.title: Interpretable Preference Dynamics: Using Neuroscientific Methods to Audit and Steer Learned Reward Models
ideas.description: This research direction proposes using neuroscience-inspired interpretability methods—such as causal mediation analysis, lesion studies, and representational similarity analysis—to audit the internal mechanisms of a learned reward or critic model that is generating dynamic preference data. Instead of applying these tools to a foundation model's knowledge representations, we apply them to the preference model's judgment circuits to understand *why* it prefers certain outputs, identify potential flaws like reward hacking or biased heuristics, and surgically edit the critic to improve its alignment. This enables the creation of a self-improving alignment pipeline where the automated preference system is itself interpretable and corrigible, moving beyond black-box optimization towards transparent and steerable value learning.

ideas.title: Dense Preference Optimization for Multimodal Alignment via Segmented Reward Modeling
ideas.description: This method overcomes the intractability of direct multimodal DPO by first learning a dense reward function that assigns preference scores to fine-grained segments of an output (patches, spectrogram frames). This model is trained on human judgments comparing these localized segments. These dense rewards are then used to programmatically construct high-quality, informative preference pairs for the full multimodal generation, which are used in a final policy optimization stage. This approach provides a stable, non-degenerate learning signal and scales alignment to complex modalities like images and video.

ideas.title: Inverse Thermodynamics: Recover a Free Energy Landscape from an Optimized Policy
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from inverse problems in statistical physics to the original seed. In thermodynamics, one can infer an energy function (like a Hamiltonian) from the observed equilibrium distribution of states. Analogously, a DPO-trained policy represents a kind of 'equilibrium' distribution over language model outputs, shaped by an implicit reward. By framing the policy's preference-ranked data and its output probabilities as observations from a system in equilibrium, we can employ inverse optimization methods (e.g., maximum entropy inverse reinforcement learning adapted for the DPO framework) to solve for the explicit reward function that, when used in the standard RLHF objective, would yield the given DPO-optimized policy. This recovers an interpretable reward model from the distilled policy, achieving the dual objective.

ideas.title: Capability via Composition: Achieving Emergent Scale through Heterogeneous Model Ecosystems
ideas.description: This research direction challenges the paradigm of scaling a single homogeneous model by instead architecting ecosystems of diverse, specialized, and often smaller models. The core innovation is to develop advanced composition mechanisms—such as learned routers, cross-model attention, or dynamic task graphs—that allow these heterogeneous components to integrate their capabilities fluidly for complex problems. The goal is to demonstrate that the emergent, systemic intelligence of such a composed ecosystem can match or exceed the broad capabilities of monolithic foundation models, but with greater efficiency, adaptability, and transparency. This achieves a form of 'effective scale' through intelligent system design rather than through raw parameter count.

ideas.title: Inverse Reinforcement Learning from DPO: Recovering Preference Distributions via Perturbation and Response Analysis
ideas.description: This research adapts the inverse problem methodology from seismic tomography to the domain of AI alignment. The core challenge is to extract the underlying human preference distribution from a model trained with Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), an inverse problem analogous to inferring Earth's subsurface structure from seismic wave data. In the analogous geophysical solution, the Earth's interior is probed with seismic waves, and its structure is inferred by analyzing how those waves are perturbed. Adapted here, the DPO-aligned model is probed with systematic perturbations—such as varying input prompts, injecting noise into intermediate activations, or applying gradient-based saliency attacks—to see how its output preferences (e.g., the probability difference between chosen and rejected responses) shift. By analyzing the correlation between these controlled input/representation perturbations and the resulting output preference perturbations across a broad query distribution, we can formulate and solve an optimization problem to recover a parameterized estimate of the original human preference model (e.g., a reward function or pairwise preference distribution) that guided the DPO training. This provides a window into the 'black box' of alignment, enabling auditing, preference debugging, and the transfer of learned preferences to other systems.

ideas.title: Preference Learning via an Adaptive, Self-Correcting Fitness Landscape
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that preference data must be static and human-provided by drawing an analogy from evolutionary biology. In ecosystems, fitness is not a fixed checklist but a dynamic outcome of continuous interaction. Adapting this, we propose a system where an AI agent and a learned critic model engage in co-evolution. The critic defines preferences, but its own success criteria are automatically updated based on the agent's developed behaviors and the long-term outcomes of those preferences. This creates a dynamic, automated, and self-correcting preference-generation mechanism, moving the learning process beyond the limitations of a fixed human dataset towards open-ended capability refinement.

ideas.title: Curriculum-Guided Reward Recovery for Iterative Policy Refinement
ideas.description: This approach begins by applying a difficulty-based curriculum to preference pairs for stable DPO training of an initial policy. From this curriculum-trained policy, an explicit and interpretable reward function is distilled using the DPO objective in reverse. The key synergy is that this recovered reward model is then used to automatically assess the difficulty or preference strength of new, unseen candidate response pairs, allowing the system to dynamically construct and advance its own training curriculum. This creates an iterative loop of policy improvement and reward refinement, where each cycle uses a progressively more sophisticated reward signal to order data, leading to faster convergence and a final reward function that is both high-quality and human-interpretable.

ideas.title: Adversarial, Closed-Loop Evaluation for Adaptive Robustness Testing
ideas.description: This research designs a closed-loop evaluation framework where foundation models are continuously tested by adaptive adversarial agents, not static datasets. The system uses automated red-teaming, where probe-generator models are trained via reinforcement learning to find and exploit new failure modes in the target model, creating a moving target for evaluation. This generates a never-ending stream of novel test cases that evolve in response to the target model's improvements, systematically uncovering emergent and strategic failures. The result is a living benchmark that measures robustness against intelligent adversaries rather than static distributions.

ideas.title: Dynamic Difficulty Curriculum for DPO via Online Difficulty Scoring
ideas.description: This approach implements a curriculum for Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) where the 'difficulty' of a preference pair is not pre-defined but dynamically estimated online based on the current model state. A difficulty scorer, which can be a small learned network or a metric derived from the training loss, continuously ranks data batches. The training scheduler then uses a sampling strategy, such as a prioritized replay or a progressively shifting mixture, that balances easy samples for stability and hard samples for learning based on real-time difficulty scores. This creates a self-adjusting curriculum that adapts to the model's evolving capabilities, maximizing sample efficiency and stabilizing training.

ideas.title: Neural Ensemble Learning via Specialized Model Ecosystems
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption of model homogeneity by proposing the design of AI ecosystems comprising diverse, smaller, specialized models that collaborate, inspired by solutions from ecology and evolutionary biology. In natural ecosystems, biodiversity and niche specialization—solved through co-evolution and symbiotic interactions—create resilient, adaptable systems. We adapt this by architecting ensembles where models with distinct capabilities (e.g., for logic, perception, or language) interact via defined protocols for mutualistic exchange, resource (computation/data) sharing, and error correction. The goal is to achieve robust, general-purpose performance not through scale homogeneity, but through engineered diversity and cooperative dynamics, potentially leading to more efficient, interpretable, and adaptable AI systems.

ideas.title: Curriculum Learning for DPO via Cognitive Load Sequencing
ideas.description: This idea adapts the principle of cognitive load sequencing from educational psychology to stabilize and accelerate Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) training. The analogous solution from instructional design involves scaffolding learning by first presenting simplified, prototypical examples to establish core schemas before gradually introducing complexity and nuance. Adapted to DPO, this means ordering the training preference pairs to create a curriculum. Training begins on a curated subset where the chosen response is demonstrably superior in fundamental dimensions (e.g., factuality, safety, instruction-following) and the rejected response contains clear errors, providing a strong, low-noise learning signal for the base reward model. As training progresses, the curriculum introduces pairs with increasingly subtle distinctions, such as stylistic preferences, nuanced ethical trade-offs, or composite instructions, where the superiority of the chosen response is less obvious. This structured progression is hypothesized to help the model learn a more robust and generalizable reward function, reducing the likelihood of early overfitting to ambiguous signals and leading to more stable training dynamics.

ideas.title: Federated Preference Optimization via Hierarchical Aggregation
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from ecology and population genetics, which deal with understanding selective pressures across massive, diverse biological populations, to the problem of scaling Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). The analogous problem is solved via hierarchical meta-analysis and federated sampling rather than centralized processing of all data. The adapted idea is to develop Federated Preference Optimization via Hierarchical Aggregation. This involves designing efficient systems where DPO is first applied locally within distinct, massive preference sources (e.g., specific tasks, cultural domains, or data providers) to compute compact preference summaries. These diverse local updates—weighted for representativeness and quality—are then aggregated through a robust hierarchical mechanism to perform a global model update. This approach bypasses the computational bottleneck of monolithic DPO training on all pairs, enabling scaling to millions of tasks and preference sources while preserving their distinct characteristics.

ideas.title: Ecosystem-Aware Online DPO: Fostering Policy Diversity via Iterative Preference Feedback
ideas.description: This research proposes an online Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) framework that models and optimizes a population of AI policies as an adaptive ecosystem. Instead of collecting preferences to steer a single policy toward a monolithic human ideal, the system iteratively gathers feedback that evaluates both the absolute quality and the relative diversity of policy responses. The optimization objective is augmented with ecological resilience concepts, rewarding policies that fill distinct functional niches and maintain a high 'response biodiversity' to prevent systemic collapse from distributional shifts or adversarial prompts. This results in a self-improving, diverse ensemble of models that is more robust and adaptable than any single model trained with standard online DPO.

ideas.title: Active Preference Learning for Online DPO via Bayesian Optimization
ideas.description: This research seed proposes an online variant of Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) that interleaves preference collection with policy optimization, inspired by active learning loops in materials science. The analogous solution from high-throughput materials discovery uses Bayesian optimization to sequentially select the most informative experiments to characterize a material property landscape. Adapting this, the research will develop an active preference learning framework where a Bayesian model of the human's reward function, informed by prior preferences, guides the selection of new prompt-response pairs for human comparison. An acquisition function (e.g., based on uncertainty or expected reward divergence) will choose comparisons that are most likely to reduce ambiguity in the reward model. The policy will then be updated via DPO on this newly collected, high-information batch, creating a closed-loop system of iterative policy improvement and targeted data collection. This aims to solve the online DPO problem with greater sample efficiency and stability than methods relying on passive or randomly sampled preference data.

ideas.title: Anchored Online DPO with Adversarial Robustness via Preference Model Ensembles
ideas.description: This work proposes a robust online DPO framework designed to prevent policy collapse from adversarial preference exploitation. The system maintains a fixed anchor dataset of high-quality human preferences and optimizes the policy with a constrained loss that limits distributional shift away from this anchor. Simultaneously, an ensemble of diverse preference models evaluates online-collected pairs, filtering out those with high disagreement or low ensemble confidence to mitigate reward hacking. This approach enables safe online preference collection while ensuring policy stability and alignment with core human values.

ideas.title: Anchored Dynamic Preference Learning with Strategic Human Oversight
ideas.description: This approach uses a learned critic model to generate dynamic preference data, but crucially prevents value drift by maintaining a formal anchor to human judgment. The system employs an uncertainty-aware arbitration module that identifies critical points—such as novel situations or high-stakes decisions—where the automated critic's preferences must be validated. At these points, it queries a human overseer not for granular labels, but for feedback on the principles or trade-offs involved. This sparse, strategic feedback is then used to iteratively correct and align the critic's underlying reward model, ensuring the dynamic system remains robustly aligned with human values.

ideas.title: Formally Verified Curriculum Learning for Preference Alignment
ideas.description: This research direction integrates formal verification directly into the curriculum learning process for Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). It uses formal methods to define and prove safety, consistency, or reasoning properties on model outputs, then uses the difficulty of satisfying these properties to automatically order preference pairs into a curriculum. This creates a self-improving loop: the curriculum trains the model on progressively harder formal constraints, while the model's improving ability to satisfy these constraints refines the difficulty assessment for subsequent curriculum stages. The result is a DPO training process that is not only more stable and sample-efficient but also provably aligned with specified formal guarantees throughout its learning trajectory.

ideas.title: Evolutionary Pressure via Curriculum Learning
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that broad AI capabilities require massive parameter scaling by drawing an analogy from evolutionary biology's method of directed evolution. In that field, desired functional capabilities in enzymes are developed not by increasing organism size, but by applying a carefully designed, escalating series of environmental challenges that select for and force the evolution of specific traits. Adapted to AI research, the solution is to fix a model's parameter count and architecture, then pursue capability gains by designing and applying a rigorous 'curriculum of challenges' during training. The training environment would be iteratively made more difficult—through progressively harder data distributions, adversarial perturbations, or compositional task demands—creating selective pressure for the model to develop more efficient, general, and robust internal representations and algorithms. The core hypothesis is that capability can emerge from the model's adaptive response to escalating environmental complexity, making challenge scale, not parameter scale, the primary driver of performance.

ideas.title: Multimodal Preference Optimization with Nuanced Feedback
ideas.description: This research direction develops a novel Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) variant for aligning multimodal generative models (for image, audio, video) by moving beyond the binary win/loss preference assumption. It formulates a loss function that can learn from more nuanced human feedback, such as continuous quality scores or partial rankings over a set of generated outputs. This allows the model to capture subtle, often subjective aspects of multimodal content like aesthetic style, temporal coherence, or emotional resonance. Consequently, it provides a more data-efficient and stable pathway to align complex generative models with detailed human judgments across diverse modalities.

ideas.title: Distributionally Robust Meta-Learning for Preference Adaptation with Uncertainty-Aware Updates
ideas.description: This research designs a meta-learning system for preference adaptation that is robust to distribution shifts in user preferences. It meta-trains a model using DPO on a broad, adversarially augmented curriculum of preference vectors to prevent overfitting to a narrow training distribution. The adaptation procedure incorporates an uncertainty estimate, allowing the system to recognize out-of-distribution users and apply more conservative, regularized updates. This results in a model that adapts quickly to in-distribution preferences while maintaining safe and stable performance for novel users.

ideas.title: Efficiently Orchestrated Model Ecosystems with a Learned Coordination Substrate
ideas.description: This research designs heterogeneous model ecosystems where a lightweight, neural orchestrator is fundamentally integrated to solve the coordination bottleneck. The orchestrator is trained end-to-end with the specialist models to predict optimal communication graphs and fusion strategies for any given input, minimizing latency and bandwidth. This transforms the ad-hoc collaboration problem into a learnable system property, enabling the ensemble to achieve superior performance and efficiency than a monolithic foundation model by leveraging specialization without being crippled by overhead.

ideas.title: Context-Aware, On-Device Alignment via Online Preference Distillation and Lightweight DPO
ideas.description: This system overcomes the static dataset limitation by enabling models to align directly with on-device user behavior. It employs a dual-component architecture: a micro reward model that distills real-time, implicit user feedback into preference scores, and a memory-optimized DPO engine that performs incremental updates using these freshly generated preference pairs. This allows the model to continuously adapt to individual user style, task requirements, and local context without external data transfer. The result is a robust, privacy-preserving alignment loop that maintains efficiency while ensuring the model remains relevant and useful in its specific deployment environment.

ideas.title: Theoretical Analysis of DPO for Domain-Specialized Preference Optimization
ideas.description: This research direction fuses the investigation of DPO's theoretical properties with the hypothesis that specialized data outperforms broad data. It formally analyzes how the optimization landscape, convergence rates, and generalization bounds of the DPO objective are fundamentally shaped by the statistical and semantic properties of the preference dataset. The core novelty is developing a theoretical framework to derive conditions—such as preference consistency, coverage, and expert signal-to-noise ratio—under which a smaller, curated domain-specific dataset provably yields better-aligned models than a larger, noisier general-purpose one. This provides principled guidance for data curation in alignment, grounding the empirical 'inversion' assumption in rigorous learning theory.

ideas.title: Formal Verification with Adaptive Specification Refinement for AI Safety
ideas.description: This system combines formal verification with an interactive specification refinement loop that addresses the fundamental challenge of creating correct and complete safety specifications for AI models. It uses runtime verification and model checking to identify gaps between formal specifications and actual desired behavior, then employs counterexample-guided abstraction refinement techniques adapted for specification improvement rather than just model refinement. The approach integrates human-in-the-loop validation of discovered specification ambiguities and automatically generates test cases that expose underspecified or contradictory requirements, creating a co-evolution of both the model's verified properties and the specifications themselves.

ideas.title: Non-Parametric Preference Learning with Gaussian Processes for Robust Pairwise Comparison
ideas.description: This research develops a robust framework for learning from pairwise comparisons that inverts the standard practice of assuming a specific parametric choice model (e.g., Bradley-Terry). Instead, it places a Gaussian Process prior directly over the latent preference or utility function, creating a flexible, non-parametric model of choice behavior. This approach can capture complex decision patterns, such as violations of transitivity or context effects, that are misspecified by standard models. The resulting system provides not only point estimates of item strength but also principled uncertainty quantification, making it robust to the true, unknown data-generating process behind human choices.

ideas.title: Meta-Contrastive Preference Optimization (MCPO)
ideas.description: This research direction proposes Meta-Contrastive Preference Optimization (MCPO), which meta-trains a model to adapt its DPO loss function itself for new users. The core innovation is that the meta-learned adaptation process specifically optimizes how to perform hard negative mining and temperature scaling within the contrastive loss, based on a small set of a new user's preference demonstrations. This allows the model to learn not just policy parameters, but a strategy for discerning that particular user's subtle preference boundaries and trade-offs from very few examples, dramatically improving personalization efficiency and robustness compared to applying either technique statically.

ideas.title: Societal Alignment via Iterative, Interdisciplinary Preference Learning
ideas.description: This research develops a novel online Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) framework where the preference data collection loop is explicitly designed as an interdisciplinary, sociotechnical process. Instead of collecting preferences from a generic or narrow set of raters, the method embeds panels of experts from diverse fields (e.g., ethics, law, social science, domain specialists) and impacted communities into the iterative training cycle. Their deliberative judgments on model outputs provide the preference pairs, ensuring the policy optimization continuously refines the model's behavior according to multifaceted societal impacts and trade-offs. This results in foundation models whose alignment is dynamically co-shaped by technical optimization and deep, ongoing societal engagement.

ideas.title: Theoretically-Grounded Curriculum Learning for DPO: Formal Analysis of Data Ordering and Optimization Dynamics
ideas.description: This research direction fuses curriculum learning for DPO with a formal theoretical analysis of the DPO objective. The core synergy is to use theoretical insights—such as the geometry of the loss landscape, gradient norms, or the sharpness of the implicit reward function—to define a principled, non-empirical measure of 'difficulty' for preference pairs. This theoretically-informed curriculum schedule is then analyzed to prove its impact on convergence rates, optimization stability, and generalization bounds. The outcome is a closed-loop framework where theory guides curriculum construction, and the resulting training dynamics provide empirical data to refine the theoretical models.

ideas.title: Immunological Data Sanitization for Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from computational immunology to enhance security and privacy in training data for foundation models. The analogous problem is the biological immune system's need to defend the body against both internal dysfunction (autoimmunity/self-attack) and external pathogens (poisoning), which it solves through mechanisms like negative selection of self-reactive cells and broad pathogen pattern recognition. The adapted idea is to develop an 'immunological' data sanitization framework. This system would implement a 'negative selection' phase to algorithmically filter out training data that matches known private or sensitive patterns (e.g., specific PII formats, copyrighted text), mimicking the prevention of autoimmuny. Concurrently, it would employ a 'pattern recognition' phase using learned embeddings of clean, representative data to establish a baseline 'self' pattern, allowing the detection of anomalous, potentially poisoned data points that deviate from this healthy pattern, thereby securing models against both privacy leaks and data poisoning attacks.

ideas.title: Meta-Learning for Preference Adaptation via Immune System-Inspired Clonal Selection
ideas.description: This idea adapts the clonal selection algorithm from computational immunology to the problem of meta-learning for user preference adaptation. In the analogous field, artificial immune systems use a process of selection, cloning, and hypermutation of antibody cells to rapidly adapt to novel pathogens. Here, we meta-train a base model with DPO across many users to build a general 'immune repertoire' of preference understanding. When encountering a new individual user's preference set (the antigen), the system performs a fast, few-step inner-loop adaptation—simulating clonal selection and hypermutation—to produce a user-specific model. High-affinity (high-performance) adapted models are maintained in a memory pool, allowing for instantaneous, personalized initialization and efficient ongoing adaptation from minimal interaction data, mirroring the immune system's ability to mount a swift, specific secondary response.
